A budget is a good place to start. Identify all outflows (inflows are not needed at this point, because we are looking at expenses only).  Next, determine which categories will not likely continue into retirement. Some loans, like a mortgage, may be paid off. Job-related expenses will stop. Transportation needs may diminish. At the least, saving for retirement will be greatly reduced, if not eliminated . Saving for children’s education probably will no longer be needed, but grandchildren’s needs may enter the picture .

Healthcare expenses could also increase . Additionally,the individual may experience other expenses.

Page 13: A: Longevity Risk  The longer you live, the more money you will need  We know that people are living longer than ever before.  A good financial advice adage is “plan for the worst and work for the best”. In this case, planning for the worst means including the longest reasonable lifespan for the client.  Family history is a good place to start. How long have family members lived? www.proschoolonline.com Page 14: 2: InflationImpact  One of the threads running through the longevity section is the potential impact of inflation. There is a substantial differencebetweenprojectionsbased on 3.5 percent inflationand one that suggestsmuch higher rates.  Currentinflation in most of the developed world is at the historically low end of the scale.

Fixed and terminable 2. Fixed and permanent 3. Variable and terminable 4. Variable andpermanent www.proschoolonline.com Page 16: 1. Fixed and terminable Fixed andTerminable:  Fixed goal amounts do not change during the funding period. • A fixed home mortgageis a good exampleof making paymentsthat do not change throughoutthe loan term. • When an individualagrees to a mortgage,the plan is often to time the mortgage repaymentperiod so that it is paid off prior to, or early in,retirement.  Paymentsfor any loans that cannot be fully repaid prior to retirementwill need to be included in the retirement budget.  As the loans are retiredrelatedpaymentscan be put to other uses, such as payingoff additional ongoing commitments.

Fixed and Permanent Fixed andPermanent:  Some goals and relatedfinancial arrangementswill not end at retirement.  The potential problem is that while payments remain at pre-retirement levels, retirement cash flow often does not.  This is a good reason to discuss ongoing financial commitments long beforeretirement.  Quite a few retirement-related concerns involve lifestyle decisions, many of which are made prior to retirement. Purchasinga bigger house or car fits into this category.  It is important for the financial advisor to work with clients to review long-term goals and financial decisions as part of early retirement planning discussions. Fixed, permanent financial decisionshave the greatest long-term impact on retirement planning www.proschoolonline.com Page 18: 3.

Variable and Terminable Variable and Terminable:  Some long-term goals have variable funding requirements, as do many that have a shorter duration. When working with clients, financialadvisorshave the most flexibilityin this area. • For example, a client may want to fund a child’s or grandchild’s education. This is an example of a goal that is variable and terminable.  Ongoing support for dependent children, aging parents or other family members is another scenario that may exist. Here, the expenses are going to be less certain than with education and the client may not know how long the funding need willcontinue.  A good time to encourage this discussion is long before the need presents itself, and if there is never a need, the client will have that much moremoney to put towardother goals.

Some of the changeswill come as a result of economicfluctuations.  Prices increaseand decrease.Necessaryservices usuallyincreasein price over time.  Food, clothing, utilities and similar expensesalso generallyincreasein price.  The client does havesome controlin this area, but no control over actual costs. www.proschoolonline.com Page 20: C: Goal Development  Havingarbitraryfinancial goals is less helpful than havingspecific goals that are tied to specific life cycles.

Management includes devising a short- or long- term strategy for acquiring and disposing of portfolio holdings. It can also include banking, budgeting, and tax services and duties, aswell.  Investment management is also known as money management, portfolio management, or wealth management.

The term most often refers to managing the holdings within an investment portfolio, and the trading of them to achieve a specific investment objective. Page 24: Pension Funds There are two primary types of pension plans: www.proschoolonline.com DEFINED BENEFIT PLAN • Defined benefit plans promise to pay a specified benefit to qualified retirees. • They do not guarantee a retirement benefit. • They provide a sum of money which the retiree can use to fund retirement cash flow based on amounts the individual has contributed, employer contributions, and investment return rates. • Often, DB plans do not require any contributions from individuals. Accounts are fully funded by the sponsor (e.g., government and/or employer). DEFINED CONTRIBUTION PLAN • Defined Contribution plans require participants to contribute to the plans.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: unded by the sponsor (e.g., government and/or employer). DEFINED CONTRIBUTION PLAN • Defined Contribution plans require participants to contribute to the plans. • In fact, without participant contribution, it’s possible the individual will not accumulate any money in the plan.  Some territories offer fully-funded DB plans (sometimes also known as a social security program ) while others have a combination of DB and DC plans, with both government funding and private/individual funding. We will look at government -sponsored pensions first, then those offered through employers Page 25: Types of Non-Pension Employee Retirement Benefits www.proschoolonline.com FINANCIAL WELLNESS BENEFITS • It can include savings, investments, debt, various types of insurance cover, funding tools (e.g., college, housing, etc.) and similar retirees. • Financial wellness can also include income tax and estate distribution guidance. MEDICAL BENEFITS • This can include regular medical benefits, extended care (long-term care), specialized, such as dental and eye care, along with other ancillary benefits.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: FITS • This can include regular medical benefits, extended care (long-term care), specialized, such as dental and eye care, along with other ancillary benefits. LIFE INSURANCE AND DEATH BENEFITS • It includes specific monthly payments to spousal and dependent children beneficiaries. • It also provides an actual life insurance policy to the retiree. • The periodic payments is supplemented both by employer and employee over a period of time. OTHER BENEFITS • Additionally, retirees may receive legal services, university tuition payments, transportation fees (e.g., public transportation), athletic facility access, and others. Some of the types of non-pension employee retirement benefits provided by the government are as follows: Page 26: Annuities INTRODUCTION: www.proschoolonline.com  In today’s world, annuities are simply another investment option, one that, in some territories, provides tax benefits. However, annuities continue to offer, as one of their most valuable options, the possibility of a lifetime income stream.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: me territories, provides tax benefits. However, annuities continue to offer, as one of their most valuable options, the possibility of a lifetime income stream.  In a time value of money context, an annuity is a stream of payments.  At its core, it was (and remains) designed to provide a stream of regular payments to the annuitant (i.e., the owner/beneficiary and recipient of the payments).  An annuity’s function is to spread invested capital and earned interest over a period – such as the life of the annuitant.  Actuaries and mortality calculations enter the picture to determine, based on age, and sometimes gender, the amount of each periodic payment to the annuitant.  When the capital and interest turn into an income stream, the contract is said to be annuitized. Page 27: Types of Annuities – Immediate AnnuityImmediate Annuities:  Immediate annuities are those where a single sum is deposited and payments to the annuitant normally begin one benefit period after the contractis issued.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: es:  Immediate annuities are those where a single sum is deposited and payments to the annuitant normally begin one benefit period after the contractis issued.  The annuity payments are either a fixed amount (immediate fixed annuity) or an amount that varies with the unit value of the underlying fund (immediate variable annuity).  An immediate annuity (annuitization) most often is purchased because the buyer wants to begin receiving a stream of regular periodic payments that are guaranteed by the insurer to last for some pre-specified period of time, generally the remaining life of the beneficiary(s) or some other pre-selected time frame.  Twovery important considerationsexist when contemplatingannuitization: www.proschoolonline.com First - Annuitization is generally irrevocable. • Once payment begins, no option exists to reverse the decision and retrieve the principal. Second - Long -term effect of inflation on purchasing power. • Inflation at four percent per year halves purchasing power in a mere 18 years.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: eve the principal. Second - Long -term effect of inflation on purchasing power. • Inflation at four percent per year halves purchasing power in a mere 18 years. Page 28: Types of Annuities – Deferred Annuity  Once the annuitant selects an annuity income option and payments begin, the annuity account is more or less frozen.  At this point the owner has used the amount accumulated in the annuity to purchase an annuity payment stream from the insurance company—so any amount previously accumulated in the annuity no longer belongs to the owner (it was “sold” to the insurance company for the income stream).  When someone says the funds in an annuity are “annuitized,”he or she is referring to this purchase. Some companies are making changes to annuities that allow for increased distribution (payout) flexibility. www.proschoolonline.com Page 29: Types of Annuities – Fixed Annuities Fixed Annuities:  In the same way that a whole life insurance contract is fixed, meaning preset premiums, cash values and death benefit, a fixed annuity contractis preset.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: ies:  In the same way that a whole life insurance contract is fixed, meaning preset premiums, cash values and death benefit, a fixed annuity contractis preset.  A fixed annuity payment period may be immediate or deferred, but either way, the amount is fixed in the contract.  Contracts almost always vary by company, so even though we can identify basic or core contract types and benefits, it is highly likely that individual insurers will offer variations .  Insurance contracts and annuities often provide options (i.e., riders, endorsements or additional features)that modify basic coverage.  As an example, we may identify that a basic fixed annuity contract provides for level payments throughout the contract period. However, the annuity may also have an option that provides for inflation (e.g., cost-of- living) adjustments.  These additional coverage options and guarantees almost always increase a contract’s expenses.  As such, you should do a cost -benefit analysis to determine which options, if any, you should recommend.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: antees almost always increase a contract’s expenses.  As such, you should do a cost -benefit analysis to determine which options, if any, you should recommend. www.proschoolonline.com Page 30: Types of Annuities – Variable Annuities VariableAnnuities:  A variable annuity may provide a solution for maintaining purchasing power , in that, depending on investment returns, annuity payments may keep pace with inflation.  If we can compare a fixed annuity to a whole life insurance policy (minus the life insurance part), we can compare a variable annuity to a variable (or VUL) life contract.  The variable annuity accumulation account invests in owner-chosen mutual funds, or their insurer equivalent.  This means that variable annuities are securities, and must be treated as such (including observing all licensing - relatedrequirements).  Some variable annuity contracts can have a lot of fees, you are correct and therefore advisors must exercise caution to determine whether related fees are appropriate or excessive.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: y contracts can have a lot of fees, you are correct and therefore advisors must exercise caution to determine whether related fees are appropriate or excessive.  Also, when trying to calculate annuity investment returns, you may see a sizable difference between gross and net returns – especially in the contract’s early years.  All annuities, other than those with immediate annuitization, have two primary periods: Accumulation phase and Annuity phase (Payout period). www.proschoolonline.com Page 31: Types of Annuities – Indexed Annuities Indexed Annuities:  Indexed annuities (IAs) offer some of the growth potential of the stock market with the downside protection of a guaranteed annuity.  Indexed annuities have characteristics of both fixed and variable annuities. They usually provide a guaranteed minimum interest rate and an interest rate tied to a market index.  They typically are linked to a benchmark, which provides the growth potential in these accounts .  The participation rate determines how much of the underlying index’s gain will be applied to the account value.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: vides the growth potential in these accounts .  The participation rate determines how much of the underlying index’s gain will be applied to the account value.  EXAMPLE: If the participation rate is 90 percent, and the S&P increases by 10 percent in a period of time (called the index interval, which can be 1, 5, 7, or even 10 years), the annuity’s account value would increase by 9 percent (90 percent of the 10 percent increase).  Some annuities also may have a rate cap, which will limit the amount of growththat can be applied to the account value for a given interval.  Note: Since these products are fairly sophisticated, so both financial advisors and their clients should have a firm understanding of these annuities before adding them to a portfolio. www.proschoolonline.com Page 32: Settlement and Pay-out Options  Annuity payment options are essentially the same as life insurance settlement options. In both cases, lump sum payment is the option most commonly used.  This gives the beneficiary all the funds at once.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: same as life insurance settlement options. In both cases, lump sum payment is the option most commonly used.  This gives the beneficiary all the funds at once. These can then be used for any purposes and serve a valuable cash flow function.  When beneficiaries choose to receive regular payments in lieu of a lump sum, the life income option is most frequently chosen. This option will make payments during the life of the beneficiary.  At death, all payments stop, regardless of whether money remains in the account or not.  As an example, assume an account has $100,000 and the beneficiary has chosen a life income (or single life) payment option. If the beneficiary dies after receiving payments of $20,000, the remaining $80,000 will stay with the insurer.  There is no recourse. This is why this option is seldom recommended , except possibly for situations in which only one individual is involved, and he or she does not wish to leave a bequest to anyone.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: ption is seldom recommended , except possibly for situations in which only one individual is involved, and he or she does not wish to leave a bequest to anyone. Settlement and Pay- out Options www.proschoolonline.com Page 33: Settlement and Pay-out Options In addition to life income, annuities also offer other options:  Joint (and survivor) – payments continue during the lifetimes of both annuitants, after which they terminate. Joint payments may remain level during both lifetimes, or may be reduced (e.g., one-half) following the death of the primary annuitant. If the annuity has a survivor option (certain jurisdictions only) any amount of the basis (i.e., capital plus earnings) that remains after the deaths of the annuitants will be paid to a beneficiary.  Period certain – annuity payments continue for at least a minimum number of years (e.g., 10). Depending on the contract terms, payments may end once the set number of years has passed, or may continue for the remaining lifetime of the annuitant.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: g., 10). Depending on the contract terms, payments may end once the set number of years has passed, or may continue for the remaining lifetime of the annuitant. If the annuitant predeceases the period certain, remaining funds go to a beneficiary.  Fixed amount – annuity payments made in a fixed amount (e.g., 1,000 pesos) for as long as the principal (and earnings) last, after which all payments stop.  Refund – depending on the payout option chosen, the contract may allow for a refund of any remaining money (principal plus earnings) left in the contract at the end of the annuity period (i.e., when the annuitant dies). Where included, the total of payments will be calculated, and if less than the total amount of principal and earnings in the contract, the remainder will be paid to a www.proschoolonline.com Page 34: Sources of Cash Flow in Retirement  One of the first things we should clarify is the subtle difference between income and cash flow. www.proschoolonline.com INCOME Income in retirement is generated by dividends or interest.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: should clarify is the subtle difference between income and cash flow. www.proschoolonline.com INCOME Income in retirement is generated by dividends or interest. CASH FLOW Cash flow can come from dividends and interest, along with resources generated from the sale of assets, including liquidation investment principal.  Cash flow provides a more broad-based source from which to generate payments to the client.  Previously, when we discussed the bucket system, we suggested placing income-producing assets in the first bucket.  This may be acceptable for high net worth individuals with enough assets to generate sufficient cash flow from dividends and interest.  The average retiree, however, will need to include money from other assets in the first bucket. Page 35: Sources of Cash Flow in Retirement www.proschoolonline.com  This money will come from selling the assets and placing the proceeds in the bucket (account), then using everything in bucket 1 to provide desired cash flow for two to three years.  Increased longevity provides more opportunity for retirees to run out of money.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: ng everything in bucket 1 to provide desired cash flow for two to three years.  Increased longevity provides more opportunity for retirees to run out of money.  The solution to providing adequate cash flow and protecting against running out of money is either to accumulate large sums or to keep as large a portion of accumulated assets as possible invested, while gradually liquidating amounts needed to provide cash flow.  The longer-term investments can help offset purchasing power problems related to increased longevity. Page 36: Retirement DistributionRates Beyond the Buckets:  The bucket system is one approach to retirement cash flow. For it to work well, relativelylarge amounts of assets are required.  Not everyone accumulates large retirement funding accounts, but they live in retirement.  Several researchers have suggested alternative approaches for a broader group of retirees.  A four-percent distribution rate is a common conservative recommendationwhen inflation is low, designed to provide cash flow and maintain some level of funding throughout retirement.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: ution rate is a common conservative recommendationwhen inflation is low, designed to provide cash flow and maintain some level of funding throughout retirement.  This means that a retiree with $100,000 in assets wouldhave to live on around $4,000 annually.  The exact withdrawal amount will depend on investment results each year , but four percent does not provide much money for those who have accumulated a relatively smallerfund. www.proschoolonline.com Page 37: Portfolio DistributionOptions  Advisors needs to understandthat that the distribution phase can be as, or even more, importantthan the accumulation phase.  An unsustainable distribution or withdrawal plan can mean the retiree’s final years may be spent in fear of not having enough money.  We may start with a four to five percent distribution scheme, but from there, the scheme would have to regularly be evaluated and modified based on current events (e.g., market returns, inflation, etc.), changes in the client’s life, shifts in goals, and any number of other factors.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: ed and modified based on current events (e.g., market returns, inflation, etc.), changes in the client’s life, shifts in goals, and any number of other factors.  It’s easy to anticipate the need to adjust portfolio distributions (and allocations)based on changes in the economicenvironment.  Some other changes, that cannot be anticipated, may have an even greater impact. www.proschoolonline.com Page 38: Impact of Taxeson RetirementCash Flow  The amount of taxation is important to retirees, because it determinesthe net benefit they receive.  Benefits from retirement plans are often fully or partially taxable when received.  EXAMPLE, there is a significant differentbetween a $1,000 monthly benefit with no taxation and a similar benefit taxed at 25 percent. The 25 percent tax results in a net benefit received of only $750, which may result in the retiree not being able to meet expenses.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp

The 25 percent tax results in a net benefit received of only $750, which may result in the retiree not being able to meet expenses.  Generally speaking, taxes on retirement benefits tend to be lower than those on regular earned income, because income is lower, putting the individuals into a lower tax bracket, but they still can significantly impact overall cash flow.  This is especially true when retirees have accumulated enough money to provide large amounts of retirement income. Impact of Taxes on Retirement Cash Flow www.proschoolonline.com Page 39: Impact of Taxeson RetirementCash Flow  What can clients do to control or reduce taxation? (ANS) The most obvious solution is to place assets into non-taxablesavings/investment vehicles.  Many territorieshave vehicles with earning that are not subject to income tax.  Equities in taxableaccounts are often subject to lower tax treatment(e.g., capital gain).  Insurance products – either life insurance or annuities – may offer reduced or no income taxation on internal growthof cash value accounts.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: g., capital gain).  Insurance products – either life insurance or annuities – may offer reduced or no income taxation on internal growthof cash value accounts.  The tax-free status of investment-related earnings varies so much from jurisdiction-to-jurisdiction that financial advisorswill be well-advised to researchand become familiarwith opportunitiesin their own territory.  Some retirementplan distributionshave no income tax, while others are taxed, but on a reduced basis.  When building a retirement portfolio, the financial advisor and client should consider the future taxability of asset location– within or outside of retirementaccounts. www.proschoolonline.com Page 40: Impact of Taxeson RetirementCash Flow  For now ,let’s assume that some investment returns will be fully taxable when withdrawn and some will be distributedtax free. What distributionrecommendationsshould the advisor make? • A big part of the answerrests in whether there is a distribution requirementat some future point.  Some tax authorities are only willing to delaytaxation,not eliminateit.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: of the answerrests in whether there is a distribution requirementat some future point.  Some tax authorities are only willing to delaytaxation,not eliminateit.  As a result, they demand that assets in tax-deferred retirement accounts begin the distribution process at a given age or after a certain number of years.  As an example, most tax-qualified retirement accounts in the U.S. require minimum distributions to begin by 1 April followingthe year in which the individual reaches age 70 ½.  Some of Japan’ sdefined contributionplan accounts must begin distributionsno later than age 70. Other programs have no required date by which distributions must begin. Whether minimum distribution requirements exist in your territorywill help determine the order in which retirees should take distributions from various account types.  Also, when one or more accounts require taking minimum distributions by a particular date, be sure the client does so. Otherwise, he or she may have to pay additionaltaxesor penalties.

Page 44: Gratuity  An employee is eligible for gratuity once he/she completes 5 years of working with an organization. It is a benefit paid under the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: An employee is eligible for gratuity once he/she completes 5 years of working with an organization. It is a benefit paid under the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972. It does not require any contributions from the employee.  This is a payment purely from the employer as an expression of his gratitude to the employee for having served long enough with his company.  However it can be paid earlier if an employee dies or becomes disabled during the course of the employment, even before completing 5 years of service. The formula for calculating gratuity is as below: Gratuity = ((Basic Pay+ DA) x 15 days x No. or years of service) / 26 • Where, DA is Dearness Allowance • 26 is the maximum number of working days in a month • The maximum gratuity paid to a central government employee is Rs. 20 lacs Government: Decisive shifting away from Defined Benefit Schemes www.proschoolonline.com Page 45: For non-government employees covered under the Payment of gratuity Act 1972, the gratuity is calculated as –  15 x Last drawn salary x No.

chunks.text: lonline.com Page 45: For non-government employees covered under the Payment of gratuity Act 1972, the gratuity is calculated as –  15 x Last drawn salary x No. of years of service /26  Where last drawn salary includes Basic, DA & commission earned  For government employees, the entire amount of gratuity is tax exempt. For non-government employees, the least of the following is exempt from tax- • Maximum amount specified by government i.e. Rs.20 lacs currently OR • Last drawn salary x No. of years of service x 15/26 OR • Actual gratuity received www.proschoolonline.com Government: Decisive shifting away from Defined Benefit Schemes Page 46: Mandatory contributory system Employee provident fund: During their working life, employees contribute part of their salary to the provident fund and the employer contributes an equivalent amount. This means the Contributions to the EPF are Exempt, the Interest earned throughout the fund’s lifetime is Exempt and the withdrawal at the end of one’s working tenure or on quitting one’s job is also Exempt.  EPFO is one of the World’s largest social security organizations with 19 crore plus member accounts.  The Central board of trustees administers 3 schemes – EPF Scheme 1952, Pension Scheme 1995, Insurance Scheme 1976.  It facilitates completely online interactions for all information and withdrawal related transactions  PF accumulations are immune from attachment by a court of law in case of legal liabilities, as per section 10 of the EPF & MP Act, 1952.  There is no restriction on the membership period.

Features of the EPS- 1. T o be eligible for EPS, an employee should be a member of the EPFO 2. The employee can draw pension provided he has reached the retirement age of 58 years and has been in employment for more than 10 years 3. An employee is eligible to push his retirement age out by 2 years to the age of 60. 4. He can also choose to withdraw his EPS at the age of 50. 5. Contributions to the EPS account can only be made by the employer .

The employer contributes 8.33% of the monthly salary to the EPS account of the individual. The Union Government offers an additional 1.16% contribution to those whose monthly salary is less than Rs. 15000. 6. If the EPS is withdrawn on quitting from one company before the completion of 10 years and a new account is opened with his next employer, the employee’s service period starts from Zero again. 7. The member becomes eligible for pension even if he becomes permanently and totally disabled. This is irrespective of the number that he has or has not served his pensionable period. www.proschoolonline.com Page 48: Employee Pension Scheme- 8. The family members are also eligible to receive the pension on behalf of the employee after his death.  The amount of pension an employee is eligible to receive is calculated using the formula- Pension = Pensionable salary (average of the last 12 months) x Pensionable service / 70  Pensionable service is the period for which the contributions have been received  Under the pensionable salary, the maximum monthly salary allowed for computation was Rs. 15000.  In April 2019, the Supreme Court of India took away this compulsion allowing the employer to contribute 8.33% of the (salary plus dearness allowance) without being capped at Rs. 1250 to the EPS.

Page 54: Universal Account Number (UAN) and Employer Portability  The UAN (the Universal Account number) is a 12 digit code allotted to every member of the EPFO - using which he can manage his Provident fund account.

chunks.text: ability  The UAN (the Universal Account number) is a 12 digit code allotted to every member of the EPFO - using which he can manage his Provident fund account.  This number is issued by the Ministry of Employment and Labour under the Government of India.  The UAN is used to identify the employee’s account with the EPFO – and is used as a reference number for all contributions and service related issues.  The UAN number of the employee remains the same throughout his lifetime.  It is portable and can be moved around with him to another employer when he moves between jobs.  The member is not dependent upon a single employer for his PF related requirements. www.proschoolonline.com Understand employee provident funds and their digital transition Page 55: Unique Permanent Retirement Account Number (PRAN) and Portability Features  PRAN Unique (Permanent Retirement account Number) is a mandatory 12 digit number allotted to an NPS subscriber.  This number remains with the subscriber throughout this lifetime and he can use this to open and operate both Tier 1 and Tier 2 NPS accounts.

Page 58: 2- Tier-II Account (Voluntary savings facility)  The Tier II account of the NPS is a second account that an NPS subscriber can open provided he already has a Tier I account active.  If the person wishes to voluntarily contribute more to the NPS savings, he can open a Tier II account (including NRIs).  The subscriber needs the PRAN number to open a TIER II account.  Minimum Rs. 1000 is needed to be deposited to open the account, subsequent contributions have to be a minimum of Rs.250.  The account enjoys no tax benefits hence the deposits or withdrawals are not tax exempt.

chunks.text: ypes of models/variants of the NPS system 1. All Citizen Model. 2. Government Sector Model. 3. Corporate Model. 4. NPS Swavalamban/NPS Lite/Atal Pension Yojana. ALL CITIZEN MODEL  The All Citizen Model is for the Common citizens of the country who are not dependent upon the government for their financial support or salary – such as self-employed individuals, professionals, business owners etc.  The Central government had initially issued the NPS for the government employees in 2004.  In 2009, the NPS was made available to all citizens of India.  Any citizen of India, resident as well as non-resident between the age of 18 and 65 years of age can apply for an NPS account. www.proschoolonline.com National Pension Systems (NPS) - PFRDA (Pension Fund) Regulations, 2015 Page 66: ATAL PENSION YOJANA(APY)  The socio-economically disadvantaged citizens fall under the purview of the Atal Pension Yojana model, these are the low income group citizens between the ages of 18 to 40 years who receive support from the government as a share in their pension contributions.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: l, these are the low income group citizens between the ages of 18 to 40 years who receive support from the government as a share in their pension contributions.  Under the APY ,the government guarantees a monthly pension for the subscribers in the range of Rs. 1000- Rs. 5000 – the subscriber can choose the amount of pension required on retirement and the contributions can be adjusted accordingly.  The age of exit and start of pension is 60 years. Therefore the minimum period of contribution for the subscriber has to be 20 years.  For those who are not covered under any social security scheme and are not Income T axpayers, the government also co-contributes 50% of the subscribers contribution to the NPS subject to a maximum of Rs. 1000 per annum.  The PFRDA chooses the pension funds as per the investment pattern specified by the government – the subscriber has no option to choose the investment pattern or the pension fund.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: the pension funds as per the investment pattern specified by the government – the subscriber has no option to choose the investment pattern or the pension fund. www.proschoolonline.com National Pension Systems (NPS) - PFRDA (Pension Fund) Regulations, 2015 Page 67: Taxability – Exempt- Exempt- Exempt (EEE) www.proschoolonline.com Explain Public Provident Fund (PPF) and other voluntary institutional retirement products E • The initial investmentin PPF is tax exempt under section 80 C up to Rs. 1.5 lacs. E • The interest earned during the tenure of the PPF is tax free E • The withdrawals and maturity amount of the PPF is also tax exempt.  The PPF enjoys the E-E-E benefit which implies that –  One can have an EPF as well as a PPF account –however for the purpose of tax-saving the total contribution towards both these accounts should not be more than Rs 1.5 lacs.  The deposits in one’s own account as well as that of a minor or a dependent of an unsound mind are clubbed to calculate the maximum permissible limit of deposit for an individual.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: ’s own account as well as that of a minor or a dependent of an unsound mind are clubbed to calculate the maximum permissible limit of deposit for an individual. Page 68: Immediate and Deferred Annuities  All insurers have to provide annuities as a key feature of their pension plans. These annuities can be on immediate or on deferred basis. www.proschoolonline.com Annuities IMMEDIATE ANNUITIES DEFERRED ANNUITIES This type of insurance annuity plan is purchased with a lump sum amount and the annuity pay-out begins immediately. This type of insurance annuity plan promises to pay the owner a regular income, or a lump sum, at some future date.  Deferred Annuity plans have two phases to their tenure – the accumulation phase and the vesting phase. ACCUMULATION PHASE VESTING PHASE The policyholder deposits premiums on a regular basis in the policy and the amount thus accumulated continues to grow till the vesting date. The policyholder can commute up to 60% of the amount thus accumulated tax free and the rest has to be used to purchase an annuity.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: o grow till the vesting date. The policyholder can commute up to 60% of the amount thus accumulated tax free and the rest has to be used to purchase an annuity. Page 69: Types – Period Certain, Life Certain and Life with Period Certain, With or Without Return of Purchase price www.proschoolonline.com Annuities Period Certain Life Certain Life With Period Certain • The annuity is payable for a guaranteed period that could be 5/10/15 years. • This annuity payment continues to the nominated beneficiary even if the original policyholder dies. • The annuities will continue for a period of the policyholder’s lifetime or the guaranteed period whichever ends later • The annuity is received periodically monthly or quarterly or annually depends on the policyholder’s choice till the lifetime of the holder. • There is no survival benefit to this type of annuity • This is a combination of life and period certain annuity, some amount of annuity will be received by the policyholder or his nominee for a certain guaranteed period irrespective of the policyholder being alive.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: nnuity, some amount of annuity will be received by the policyholder or his nominee for a certain guaranteed period irrespective of the policyholder being alive. • The other part of the annuity will be received by the policyholder till his lifetime Page 70: www.proschoolonline.com Annuities Life Annuity With The Return Of Purchase Price: Life Annuity Without The Return Of Purchase Price: • Annuity is payable to the policyholder for his entire lifetime. However on death, there is no survival benefit hence the premiums paid to purchase the policy are not returnable. • The policy holder will receive annuity during his lifetime from the policy and after his death, the nominee will receive the premiums paid during the policyholder’s lifetime as death benefit.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: annuity during his lifetime from the policy and after his death, the nominee will receive the premiums paid during the policyholder’s lifetime as death benefit. Types – Period Certain, Life Certain and Life with Period Certain, With or Without Return of Purchase price  Various insurers have devised other plans which include some return of the purchase price to beneficiaries post the policyholder’s death – to the extent of 50%, 75% or the balance that remains once the policyholder is no more.  There is also an option where the annuity can be continued to be paid to the joint or last survivor till his/her lifetime without the return of the purchase price.  An inflation-adjusting annuity payment is also an option available with some insurers wherein the annuity payment increases with every year to counter the effects of inflation on the annuity payout.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: is also an option available with some insurers wherein the annuity payment increases with every year to counter the effects of inflation on the annuity payout. Page 71: Government sponsored regular income schemes 1- Senior Citizens Savings Scheme (SCSS):  The government of India runs the Senior Citizens Savings Scheme as a sovereign guaranteed monthly income scheme for its senior citizens aged above 60 years.  The rate of interest on the scheme is also fixed by the government from time to time –the recent rate is 7.4% p.a. Features of Senior Citizens Savings Scheme (SCSS)– 1. Only one deposit can be done in an account – only lump sums are allowed. For further deposits, new accounts need to be opened. 2. A joint account can only be opened with a spouse and there is no age limit applicable for the second applicant. 3. Designated Private and Public sector banks and post offices offer this facility. 4. One can have multiple accounts so far as the combined deposits do not exceed Rs. 15 lacs. 5. The scheme has a maturity period of 5 years which can be extended by up to 3 years. 6.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: le accounts so far as the combined deposits do not exceed Rs. 15 lacs. 5. The scheme has a maturity period of 5 years which can be extended by up to 3 years. 6. The extension request has to be made within 1 year of the completion of the first 5 years. www.proschoolonline.com Page 72: Features (Continued) 6. Minimum deposit is Rs. 1000 and the deposit has to be in multiples of Rs. 1000 and the upper limit is Rs. 15 lacs. 7. Individuals below the age of 60 who have retired early after the age of 55 under the VRS (voluntary retirement scheme) or Superannuation scheme can also open an SCSS account. 8. Retired defence personnel can open this account irrespective of the age limit. 9. NRIs, PIOs and HUFs are not eligible to open this account. 10. The deposits in the SCSS are eligible for tax deduction under section 80C up to a limit of Rs. 1.5 lacs. 11. The interest on the SCSS is fully taxable. 12.

Post extension the depositor can withdraw anytime without penalty after 1 year of extension. 14. The interest is paid out quarterly directly into the depositor’s account www.proschoolonline.com Government sponsored regular income schemes Page 73: Post Office Monthly Income Scheme (POMIS)  Post offices in the country also run monthly income schemes recognized by the Ministry of Finance that can be used by Senior citizens to garner a scheduled monthly payment over their retirement years.  The rate currently being offered is 6.6% p.a. Features of a Post office POMIS- 1. The investment period is 5 years and locked in for 5 years. 2.

chunks.text: rement years.  The rate currently being offered is 6.6% p.a. Features of a Post office POMIS- 1. The investment period is 5 years and locked in for 5 years. 2. The interest payment is disbursed monthly. 3. An upper limit of Rs. 4.5lacs and a lower limit of Rs. 1500 applies to all deposits. 4. The upper limit applies aggregated across multiple accounts opened under POMIS. The maximum limit under a joint account is Rs. 9 lacs. The amount has to be in multiples of Rs. 1000. 5. There can be maximum of 3 joint holders but NRIs are not eligible to hold POMIS deposits. 6. Premature withdrawals are allowed after 1 year albeit with a penalty – between 1 and 3 years the penalty is 2% of the withdrawn amount and between 3and 5 years, the penalty is 1%. 7. A minor can also have a POMIS account – the upper limit for deposit in this case is Rs. 3 lacs. 8. No TDS Is deducted on the interest payments.

No TDS Is deducted on the interest payments. www.proschoolonline.com Government sponsored regular income schemes Page 74: Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972 ➢ The payment of gratuity Act, 1972 is applicable to all employees engaged in factories, mines, oilfields, plantations, ports, railway companies, shops, and other establishments where 10 or more workers are employed. Applicability of the Act: Establishments to which the Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972 applies- ⮚ Factories, oilfields, mines, plantations, ports, railway companies which employee more than 10 workers. ⮚ All shops and establishments in which more than 10 employees are employed. ⮚ Motor transport undertakings, clubs, chambers of commerce, institutions, societies, trusts, circus industry in which more than 10 employees are employed. ⮚ All other such establishments as notified by the Central government from time to time in which more than 10 workers are employed.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: n 10 employees are employed. ⮚ All other such establishments as notified by the Central government from time to time in which more than 10 workers are employed. www.proschoolonline.com NOTE: ➢ Even if the number of employees drops below 10, the act continues to apply to an organization where the act has ever applied. ➢ An employee is eligible for Gratuity when he completes 5 years of continued service. Page 75: Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972 Applicability of the Act ( Continued) An employee is said to be in continuous service if – ⮚ He/she has been in continued uninterrupted service including service which may be interrupted due to sickness, leave, layoff, strike, lockout or cessation of work due to no fault of the employee ⮚ He/she is employed with a seasonal establishment and he has worked for not less than 75% of the days for which the establishment was in operation during such period. In case of termination of service and re-employment, the service is considered to beinterrupted and hence not continuous service.

chunks.title: 5. Retirementplanning Ifp
chunks.text: operation during such period. In case of termination of service and re-employment, the service is considered to beinterrupted and hence not continuous service. www.proschoolonline.com The payment of eligible gratuity becomes due when the employee – Resigns or retires from active service On superannuation On his death or disability NOTE: It can also be paid prior to completion of 5 years if the employee dies or becomes disabled due to accident or disease. Page 76: Special Provisions and Calculation of Gratuity payable on Death ➢ Gratuity payable depends upon two factors- – Numbers of years of service – Last drawn salary ➢ However, in case of death of employee, the gratuity is calculated and paid based on the length of the service and the maximum benefit is restricted to Rs. 20lacs.

For eg: 7 years and 8 months will be considered as 8 years.

For eg: 7 years and 8 months will be considered as 8 years. ➢ It implies that the employee will receive 15 days of salary for every completed year of service and the salary parameter used for this calculation would be the last drawn salary. ➢ Last drawn salary includes the basic salary, dearness allowance and commission earned.

Page 78: Tax-exempt Amount of Gratuity for Employees covered under the Act, and otherwise To calculate the amount of gratuity payable, the payment of gratuity act, 1972 has divided employees into two types- www.proschoolonline.com For employees covered under the payment of Gratuity act For employees not covered under the payment of Gratuity act Sr. No. Particulars (least of the following shall be tax exempt) 1 Last salary drawn (Basic + Dearness Allowance) x number of years of service x 15/26 2 Rs. 20 lacs 3 Actual gratuity amount received Sr. No. Particulars (least of the following shall be tax exempt) 1 Last 10 month’s average salary drawn (Basic + Dearness Allowance) x number of years of service x 15/30 2 Rs.

The remaining has to be compulsorily used to purchase annuity ⮚ Commutation of pension implies that the employee on retirement is withdrawing some amount of the pensions becoming due to him for the rest of his lifetime as a one time lump sum withdrawal.

chunks.text: s that the employee on retirement is withdrawing some amount of the pensions becoming due to him for the rest of his lifetime as a one time lump sum withdrawal. ⮚ The formula for commutation is as below – ➢ Where, commutation factor will be as per the pensioner’s table and the reference age for the commutation factor that table will be the age on his next birthday www.proschoolonline.com Commuted Value of pension = 40% x Commutation factor x 12 X Pension ordered Page 80: Employees’ Deposit Linked Insurance Scheme (EDLIS) ⮚ The employee deposit linked insurance scheme is an insurance cover provided by the Employee Provident fund organization (EPFO) for private sector salaried employees – wherein the nominee or legal heirs of the employee are entitled to receive a lump sum insurance claim from the EPFO in case of death of the member during service. ⮚ The objective of the scheme is to provide financial security to the family members of the employees covered under this scheme.

The minimum pay-out under EDLI insurance is Rs. 2.5 lacs and the maximum is Rs. 7 lacs Page 81: Employees’ Deposit Linked Insurance Scheme (EDLIS) ⮚ Features of the EDLI – 1. The age group of the employees covered is between 18 to 85 years of age 3. The employer contribution is 0.5% of employee’s basic pay (including Dearness Allowance) per month as EDLI insurance premium, maximum up to Rs. 75 per month 4. Inflow from work, which is usually stable • Salaries and wages from employment • Income from self-employment 2. Other inflows, which can fluctuate • Interest and dividend income earned • Rental income • Tax refunds • Withdrawals from savings accounts • Proceeds from sale of personal assets • Income from grants, trusts, gifts, allowances, etc. www.proschoolonline.com ➢ Outflows (i.e. Expenses) are any funds going out from an individual 1. Fixed expenses, which remain the same over a given period • Loan payments, Insurance premiums • Utility bills, OTT bills etc • School fees, recurring healthcare etc 2.

Discretionary expenses, also called Variable expenses *, which vary over time • Restaurants and entertainment • Gifts and charitable contributions • Holidays • Medical expenses (one offs) * • Shopping, parties etc Page 4: Theory Q: Describe Cashflow statement • The cashflow statement helps us to understand the net cash surplus (or deficit) • It can be for an individual or a family (i.e., sum of family members) • It indicates whether a family is living within their means (i.e. cash positive) Income Expenses Savings Income examples • Income from work • Other incomes Expense examples • Fixed expenses • Variable expenses Net position • Has a surplus • Living within means • Is losing money • Not living within means Page 5: Theory Q: How does time impact a person’s cash flow? • When life changes, money is in motion. • Sometimes these changes are subtle and small. Other times, the changes can be substantial.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: pact a person’s cash flow? • When life changes, money is in motion. • Sometimes these changes are subtle and small. Other times, the changes can be substantial. • Examples of life transitions that can affect a client’s CF include – Positive changes: – Work income / salary increase, – Investment returns increase, – receives inheritance / gift – Negative changes: • Illness or major home repairs. • Job loss or other income reduction. • Excessive spending patterns or increase in credit purchases • Divorce, separation or death of a spouse or partner. • Gambling addiction or alcohol or substance abuse. www.proschoolonline.com Page 6: Theory Q: Describe Net worth statement • A client’s net worth is a snapshot of their overall financial health at a point in time. • It is usually updated every monthly or quarter, and value on that date is take for calculation purposes Assets Liabilities Net worth What is owned • Cash or items with saleable value, • Cash • Fixed deposits • Stocks owned • Assets (e.g. car, home etc) What is owed • Amounts which still have to be repaid, e.g.

chunks.text: sh or items with saleable value, • Cash • Fixed deposits • Stocks owned • Assets (e.g. car, home etc) What is owed • Amounts which still have to be repaid, e.g. • Home loan outstanding • Car loan • Education loan • Credit card dues = Financial health • Positive • Assets > liabilities • Has money • Negative • Liabilities > Assets • Owes money Page 7: ➢ Assets are classified under three types * Some of these may have a small “liquidation” cost, but still treated as cash equivalents ** Invested and End-use assets classification depends on the client’s planned use of that asset, e.g. • A client who collects paintings would not plan to sell them (= Used) whereas an investor who buys paintings (= Investment) would sell the paintings when the price is right • A car may be classified as end-use for a few years and then moves to Invested when the owned wants to sell it Theory Q: What are the types of personal assets 1. Liquid • Cash or cash equivalents • Cash at home or in bank • Uncashed cheques • Cash equivalents * like • Bank certificates • Money market funds • Insurance policies 2.

chunks.text: Cash or cash equivalents • Cash at home or in bank • Uncashed cheques • Cash equivalents * like • Bank certificates • Money market funds • Insurance policies 2. Invested • Assets that the client intends to sell at some point, e.g. • Stocks, bonds • Commodities • Real estate • Paintings, jewellery etc** 3. Used • Assets that the client is using, i.e. not intended for sale, e.g. • Own home • Car, boat • Paintings, jewellery** Page 8: Theory Q: What are types of personal liabilities ➢ Liabilities are any outstanding amounts that the client owes ➢ These are classified as • Short-Term, if they need to be repaid within a year • E.g. personal loan from a bank, or an outstanding carried over on a credit card • Long-Term, if they are to be repaid over more than one year • Home loans, car loans etc • Here the total amount including any penalties and interest is takes as outstanding ➢ Liabilities are different from expenses, e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: • Home loans, car loans etc • Here the total amount including any penalties and interest is takes as outstanding ➢ Liabilities are different from expenses, e.g. • Monthly EMI of Rs 10,000 to pay a car loan is an Expense • Remaining car loan outstanding of Rs 1,00,000 is a Liability Page 9: Theory Q: 5 key personal finance ratios • Liquid assets as a % of total assets • Ideal is 15% of total assetsLiquidity ratio • # of months that liquidity can support • Ideal is 3 to 6 months of reservesLiquidity (Job cover) • Outstanding Debt as a % of total assets • Ideal is below 50%Debt asset ratio • Debt repayment as a % of income • Ideal is below 35%. Anything over 45% is a concernDebt service ratio • Savings as a % of income • Ideal is at least 10%Savings ratio Page 10: Theory Q: Steps to create a budget www.proschoolonline.com Review each category of income and expense as a percentage of the total.

chunks.text: st 10%Savings ratio Page 10: Theory Q: Steps to create a budget www.proschoolonline.com Review each category of income and expense as a percentage of the total. If 10,000 not saved, change line items basis actuals to ensure 10,000 Compare actual versus plan to see what is under or over the forecast Record actual incomes and expenses over the period Estimate Income and expenses from all sources and make a forecast Identify target from the budgeting exercise, e.g. save 10,000 per month Page 11: Theory Q: How can clients track their monthly budgets? www.proschoolonline.com Approach 1: Maintain records Approach 2: Envelope approach Set aside money as per plan in different envelopes Use this to pay each type of bill. Record overruns on envelope if it runs out Page 12: Theory Q: Advantages & Disadvantages of Budgeting Some advantages of budget: 1. Identifying unnecessary expenses 2. Identifying areas of family conflict over spending priorities 3. Providing an opportunity for families to discuss spending priorities 4.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: unnecessary expenses 2. Identifying areas of family conflict over spending priorities 3. Providing an opportunity for families to discuss spending priorities 4. Increasing the likelihood of achieving financial goals www.proschoolonline.com Some disadvantages of budget: 1. The process can be laborious. 2. Clients may be embarrassed by some of their spending habits and not keep accurate records. 3. People often do not like the structured approach of tracking inflows and outflows. Page 13: Theory Q: What are Conflicting Demands / SMART goals • Conflicting demands are s where the client may want to pursue multiple goals, however, due to limited budgets achieving all goals is unviable • In effect, the client needs to prioritize and select only a few goals to focus on.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: iple goals, however, due to limited budgets achieving all goals is unviable • In effect, the client needs to prioritize and select only a few goals to focus on. ➢ SMART (acronym) is a tool to improve goal setting • Specific: It must clearly state one concrete intent, e.g., pay for Education • Measurable: It must be quantified, e.g., Rs 75 lacs • Actionable: Actions can be taken to achieve the goal, e.g., investments possible • Realistic: Expectations need to be realistic, e.g., can’t expect 100% ROI • Timebound: The goal has a time limit, e.g., in 2 years • www.proschoolonline.com Page 14: Theory Q: What are General reserves meant for • Are meant for Special needs such as – Marriage, – Divorce or remarriage, – Charitable giving, – Dealing with ongoing medical expenses for exceptional illnesses, – Providing support for adult dependents (e.g., elderly parents) or – Children with ongoing medical needs.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: h ongoing medical expenses for exceptional illnesses, – Providing support for adult dependents (e.g., elderly parents) or – Children with ongoing medical needs. • Such situations will require access to emergency funds if not already planned for as part of regular goals • This means, in addition to normal budgeting, the client wants to provide added funding to meet their special needs. www.proschoolonline.com Page 15: Theory Q: What are Specific reserves meant for Emergency funds that are aimed at two uncertain risks, since they can be catastrophic Loss of Income • Complete loss of income e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: Specific reserves meant for Emergency funds that are aimed at two uncertain risks, since they can be catastrophic Loss of Income • Complete loss of income e.g. Job loss for any reason • Partial loss of income due to Illness, disability or any other reason • Can be planned for via creation of a fund Loss of residential property • Applicable to property owners • Can be catastrophic • Provisions to repair or replace the same is needed • Usually insurance plus a emergency fund is needed Page 16: Theory Q: Ways to increase income www.proschoolonline.com Here are a few possibilities for increasing income: Ask for a raise at work or ask for more responsibility that might provide a higher salary. If doing shift work, ask to work an additional shift. Find a second job, possibly part-time, to supplement income. Get a new job with a higher salary. Shift investments from a growth focus to an income focus. Income-producing investments include real estate, bonds and dividend-paying equities.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: a higher salary. Shift investments from a growth focus to an income focus. Income-producing investments include real estate, bonds and dividend-paying equities. Complete an education program to qualify for a better paying job, or a higher salary from the current job. Rent out a room in the home. Start a new or part-time business (i.e., become an entrepreneur). Page 17: Theory Q: How to build Income generating portfolio www.proschoolonline.com Two primary options exist for an income-generating portfolio – capital utilization and capital preservation (or conservation). Capital Utilization Method • Capital utilization uses existing capital in addition to earnings to generate income. • The upside of capital utilization is that it requires less capital. • The big downside is that available capital decreases, and can eventually be depleted. • This of this as Mutual fund SWP where a portion of Principal + Interest both are withdrawn. Capital Preservation Method • Capital preservation uses no capital.

chunks.text: This of this as Mutual fund SWP where a portion of Principal + Interest both are withdrawn. Capital Preservation Method • Capital preservation uses no capital. • The entire payment is the result of income / interest generated • This is the application of our Perpetuity formula where the principal stays intact. • Only the interest is withdrawn and utilized for emergency. • This method would require higher capital since only the income is withdrawn.

Theory Q: Hire Purchase / Leasing www.proschoolonline.com • Leasing is an alternative to buying • wherein the owner of the asset (lessor) permits another person (lessee) to use the asset, for recurring payments. • Leases are of two types as follows • Closed-end lease (i.e. Operating) = Operate the asset for a period then return it to owner . • Open-end Lease (i.e. Finance / Equity)= Use the asset for a period and get ownership at end of tenure on payment of any difference in the sale price of the asset and the leased amount. Page 26: Theory Q: Bankruptcy • Not all territories have bankruptcy laws. Where they exist, they generally allow for some methods to address severe debt situations. 1.

chunks.title: Cfp Ips Pfm
chunks.text: : Theory Q: Bankruptcy • Not all territories have bankruptcy laws. Where they exist, they generally allow for some methods to address severe debt situations. 1. debt forgiveness: After the bankruptcy petition is approved, debts are eliminated • Certain debts can never be forgiven, e.g., child support, college loans etc 2. debt liquidation: Certain assets are liquidated, although some may be retained 3. Many creditors prefer debt restructuring to bankruptcy. • Creditors agree to accept lower payments, by doing one of more of the following • by extending the repayment term, • forgiving part of the debt, • lowering interest rates..

Conflicts of interest: Part of disclosure, the intermediary is expected to be able to identify potential conflicts, manage the same and do a full disclosure to client www.proschoolonline.com Page 6: Due Diligence Definition: “a measure of prudence, responsibility and diligence that is expected from, and ordinarily exercised by, a reasonable and prudent person under the circumstances.” For financial advisors: ➢ Due diligence is the process of systematically verifying the accuracy of information provided by the client, ➢ Ensuring that the requirements of relevant legislation have been complied with, ➢ Full disclosure had been made to the client, ➢ And that a full record of conversations and justification of decisions made by the financial advisor are recorded in the client file.

Those territories and organizations will often hold to a suitability standard. • Suitability: based on an assessment of client requirements. Means the advisor can equally choose to recommend an investment option that pays more commission, or one that pays less. • widely recognized that fiduciary-requirement is the greater while suitability is the lesser .

Identifying the customer and verifying that customer’s identity using reliable, independent source documents, data or information. 2. Identifying the beneficial owner, i.e., the natural person(s) who ultimately owns or controls a customer and/or the natural person on whose behalf a transaction is being conducted. The Financial Institution should take reasonable measures to verify the identity of the beneficial owner 3. Obtaining information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship. 4.

Conducting ongoing due diligence on the business relationship and scrutiny of transactions undertaken throughout the course of that relationship to ensure that the transactions being conducted are consistent with the institution’s knowledge of the customer, their business, and risk profile, including, where necessary, the source of funds. Regulators are the executive branches of the government and are accountable to the legislature • They are statutory bodies (i.e. by statute) run by a board of directors, who are appointed by the MOF • They charge fees to the industry participants to cover their operational costs, thus retaining a level of financial independence MOF Regulators Page 22: Indian regulatory landscape • RBI, IRDA, SEBI are apex regulators • RBI also regulates NABARD, SIDBI • State governments have some co-regulatory powers also Page 23: Reserve Bank of India ➢ Established on April 1, 1935, in accordance with the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934. ➢ It performs merchant banking functions for the Central and State Governments. ➢ It is also a banker to all scheduled banks in India by maintaining banking accounts ➢ It regulates all commercial banks, UCB’s, NBFC’s and institutions like NABARD and NHB (who in turn regulate rural and cooperative banks) ➢ RBI is also the monetary authority.

Page 25: RBI: Board of Financial Supervision (BFS) ➢ The Board was constituted in November 1994 as a committee of the Central Board of Directors of the Reserve Bank of India. ➢ The Board is constituted by co-opting four Directors from the Central Board as members for a term of two years & is chaired by the Governor. The Deputy Governors of the Reserve Bank are ex-officio members.
