chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: Page 1: Estate Planning Page 2: Estate planning definition & timing Process of developing strategies for management and distribution of wealth leading up to and after death Estate planning usually happens in this period When is planning done Page 3: How estates are distributed On death No Will (Intestate) Succession Laws (as applicable) Distribution (Court order) Has Will (Testate / Testamentary) Probate process (Probate court) Distribution (Probate order) Exceptions (e.g., life insurance) Page 4: Key terms in Estate planning Intestate • Dying without a Will Testate • Dying with a Will Testator (or Testatrix) • Male (or Female) adult who makes a Will Legatee / Beneficiary • A person inheriting under a Will Probate • Legal process relating to administering a Will Estate • Property of a person Wills, Trusts, Gifts etc • Tools to transfer estate to others Will • Last wishes of a person, signed and attested by 2 witnesses Succession Laws • Laws that govern estate distribution Executor • Person who is appointed in a Will to administer it Page 5: Steps in Estate Planning 1.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: tnesses Succession Laws • Laws that govern estate distribution Executor • Person who is appointed in a Will to administer it Page 5: Steps in Estate Planning 1. Establish Estate: what is available with the client (Estate) 2. Identify goals, e.g., who should receive (Inheritance & Interest) 3. Understand succession Laws (Indian & international) 4. Select distribution vehicles (Will, Gifts, Trust etc) 5. Appoint fiduciary / legal representatives (Legal aspects) Page 6: 1. Establish size of Estate Page 7: Estate definition ➢ All rights, titles and interests of a person less any outstanding liabilities ➢ Also called Property. ➢ Property can be of three types as below www.proschoolonline.com Real estate / immovables • Own home(s) • Land • Shops and other owned establishments Personal property • Tangibles like vehicles jewellery, art, electronics, books etc.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: ate / immovables • Own home(s) • Land • Shops and other owned establishments Personal property • Tangibles like vehicles jewellery, art, electronics, books etc. • Intangible like securities, mutual funds, insurance, copyrights, trademarks, Royalty incomes Community property • Special category where rights or titles of assets / liabilities are shared with spouse (or similar) Page 8: Immovable Property: ownership types Individual • Also called fee simple (or freehold) • Single owner • Transfer via will, else via mandate (i.e., court order) Joint tenants • Owned in equal shares • On death of one owner, that owner’s share will pass to the other owner Tenants in common • Owned in unequal shares • Each owner can independently distribute their own share in any fashion they want Community property • Also called marital property • Allowed in some countries • All property acquired after marriage is held in a matrimonial trust ➢ Four broad ownership patterns can exist for real estate ➢ Specific rules relating to distribution are subject to Local laws Page 9: Intangible: Bank Accounts ➢ Solely o

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: ad ownership patterns can exist for real estate ➢ Specific rules relating to distribution are subject to Local laws Page 9: Intangible: Bank Accounts ➢ Solely owned bank account can be accessed by valid nominee basis documentation ➢ A joint bank account is allowed in India and permits survivor to continue account www.proschoolonline.com Page 10: Intangibles: Mutual Funds • Mutual fund units may be also held jointly, (up to 3 holders) and operated as • either or survivor, • any or survivor, • jointly • All mutual Funds require a nomination for each unitholder. • A nominee will be entitled to the units only on the death of the holder, or if there are more than one, then on the death of all holders. • If there are no joint holders, and no nominees, transmission would be in favour of legal heir(s). • Notwithstanding holding pattern / nominees, a unit holder is entitled to bequeath mutual fund units by will (subject to the applicable law of succession).

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: s). • Notwithstanding holding pattern / nominees, a unit holder is entitled to bequeath mutual fund units by will (subject to the applicable law of succession). • ‘Transmission’ is the process of units being transferred to the surviving (joint) unit holders, subject to documents needed by the asset management company or AMC www.proschoolonline.com Page 11: Intangible: Securities • For physical shares, transmission to the other joint holder, or if single, to the nominee or as advised by succession certificate / probate /court decree etc • For dematerialized share, transmission requires the surviving joint holder(s), nominee(s) or legal heir(s) to approach the Depository Participant (DP) • When no nominee + intestate + sole holder, • if the value of such shares per listed company does not exceed INR 200,000, • a simple no-objection certificate is required from all legal heirs in favor of the claimant to such shares, • Or a family settlement executed and notarized by all legal heirs of the deceased, each of which must be submitted together with an indemnity bond.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: uch shares, • Or a family settlement executed and notarized by all legal heirs of the deceased, each of which must be submitted together with an indemnity bond. www.proschoolonline.com Page 12: Life Insurance • Life Insurance is payable on death. • Payment is made to the nominee. • Insurance Act, 1938 requires that a policyholder may nominate a person or persons to receive the money from the policy on death. • A nominee can be made (or changed) by the policyholder anytime during the validity of a policy. Minors require guardians to be nominated. • Legal heirs can contest payment to a nominee if it is not in accordance with the applicable law of succession (Supreme Court order) • The Insurance (Amendment) Act, 2015, introduced the concept of beneficial nominee, i.e., the person who ultimately benefits or owns the insurance money. www.proschoolonline.com Page 13: 2. Identify goals Page 14: Primary purpose of Estate plan 1.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: ., the person who ultimately benefits or owns the insurance money. www.proschoolonline.com Page 13: 2. Identify goals Page 14: Primary purpose of Estate plan 1. Fulfil last wishes • Can be difficult to identify as each person’s situation is unique • Goals involve • a philosophical aspect (e.g., what is your legacy, meaning of your life) • a technical aspect (e.g., are heirs minors / elderly, do they need protection) 2. In the most efficient manner • Requires tax planning and other aspects to ensure efficient estate management Page 15: Strategic aspects of Estate planning Estate planning will additionally need to solve one of more of the following concerns 1. Lack of liquidity : To meet last rites and other settlement costs 2. Improper disposition of assets : Prevent assets from going to the wrong people, at the wrong time, or in the wrong manner 3. Inadequate income or capital : Insufficient for ongoing retirement expenses, client’s disability or incapacity, and family living expenses after the individual’s death 4.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: income or capital : Insufficient for ongoing retirement expenses, client’s disability or incapacity, and family living expenses after the individual’s death 4. Asset values destabilized / not maximized : Keep business valuations as high as possible and assets distributed in ways that do not reduce their value 5. Excessive taxes and transfer/distribution costs : keep expenses / taxes to minimum 6. Special needs : Provide ongoing care for a spouse who is not financially capable, children or parents who are not able to care for themselves (e.g., mental or physical disability), and support for exceptionally gifted children www.proschoolonline.com Page 16: Common client goals (i.e. last wishes) A. Provide for loved ones • Most common goal • Includes spouse or life partners, adult children etc B. Providing for children / grandchildren • Focusses on minors who are currently not equipped to handle finances • Important for HNI’s C.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: rs, adult children etc B. Providing for children / grandchildren • Focusses on minors who are currently not equipped to handle finances • Important for HNI’s C. Philanthropy • Covers charity or gifts to NGO’s, institutes, servants, care givers etc D: Small business continuity • Covers how client’s own business will transition to new owners Page 17: A.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: gifts to NGO’s, institutes, servants, care givers etc D: Small business continuity • Covers how client’s own business will transition to new owners Page 17: A. Providing for significant other ➢ Applies to: Married couples, adult with life partner (other than spouse) ➢ Goal: Ensure loved ones are provided on death or incapacity • Passing of assets • immediate liquidity to ensure lifestyle is maintained, including support for dependents ➢ Requirement: • Need for liquid assets that can be immediately accessed • Need to set aside a larger amount for spouse ➢ Liquid assets: • Life insurance (bypasses Probate) • Trusts (where allowed by law) ➢ Key consideration • Legal rights of life-partner (other than spouse) may not exist or can be contested by legal heirs in some countries Page 18: Common recipients (significant other) Spouse Is a recipient, by law Wills cannot be used to disinherit the spouse Unmarried partner May not have same legal rights as spouse. In such cases, Will may be contested by legal heirs Live-in relationship May not have any legal rights.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: arried partner May not have same legal rights as spouse. In such cases, Will may be contested by legal heirs Live-in relationship May not have any legal rights. Hence, joint holding of assets or a Trust is a good solution Ex-spouse Usually, ex- spouses have no legal right to inheritance. Page 19: B. Children / Grandchildren ➢ Applies to: Any estate owner with children / grandchildren ➢ Goal: Ensure • Dependent children are taken care of till they reach adulthood • Provide assets to them when they reach adulthood ➢ Requirement: • Provide funds for basic food, clothing and living expenses • Identifying a caregiver to oversee and guide the minors till they reach adulthood • Identifying administrators to oversee asset distribution (e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: ng expenses • Identifying a caregiver to oversee and guide the minors till they reach adulthood • Identifying administrators to oversee asset distribution (e.g. for education etc) ➢ Elements: • Childcare needs to be discussed as a family so identifying who would take up this role (of legal guardian) is best done early, even as soon as children are born • Trust administrators need to be setup to oversee the estate and ensure assets are available when needed Page 20: Common recipients (children, grandchildren) Children Common recipient, irrespective of parent’s relationship with biological mother Lifetime gift & testament transfer or both usually used Grandchildren Treated same as children usually Skip person / Direct skip Gift skips one generation, e.g., direct to grandchild Exceptionally gifted Likely to have special needs, relating to their gift (e.g., music, art, sports) In addition to money, experts from their field are sometimes appointed as guardians Page 21: C: Organizations and philanthropy ➢ Applies to: HNI’s or others who want to give back ➢ Goal: Arises from desire to leave

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: etimes appointed as guardians Page 21: C: Organizations and philanthropy ➢ Applies to: HNI’s or others who want to give back ➢ Goal: Arises from desire to leave a legacy / make a difference / stand for something • Provide charity to organization(s) of choice ➢ Requirement: • Applies once loved ones and children are taken care of ➢ Execution: • Local laws relating to taxation of gifts / donations and types of charities / NGO’s apply • Some territories allow for both testamentary or inter vivos charitable gifts to be fully or partially tax-free, while others do not. • Even where there is no taxation, the amount that can pass tax-free may be limited. Page 22: D.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: e gifts to be fully or partially tax-free, while others do not. • Even where there is no taxation, the amount that can pass tax-free may be limited. Page 22: D. Small business owners ➢ Applies to: Any estate owner who owns a small business ➢ Goal: Reduce anxiety relating to small business on death via • Keeping the business running after death • Generating income to support the family after death ➢ Requirement: • Distribute ownership in such a way that goals are achieved ➢ Aspects to consider How to keep business running / Generate income? Keep within family ? Succession plan When Transition / how Exit / Sell ? Whom, when, value Page 23: High Net Worth Individuals: Strategies • HNWI most likely will want to ensure that taxes are minimized, probate is avoided, and inheritance rights for their chosen heirs are protected. When estate taxes apply, estates of HNWI are almost certain to be impacted with a higher than average tax burden • In addition, many HNWI individuals would prefer to keep their information, especially their net worth, private.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: impacted with a higher than average tax burden • In addition, many HNWI individuals would prefer to keep their information, especially their net worth, private. Probate makes a lot of information available to the public. Net worth and assets distributed to heirs is among the possible public information. • Hence HNI’s may look to transfer assets via gifts prior to Death. • Risk of unscrupulous individuals: They can pose as creditors, present high-return, high- security investment plans, and otherwise try to get heirs to give them as much of their inheritance as possible. • To protect, They may use Trusts or alternatively use foreign (e.g., offshore) corporations to hold assets, or domestic corporate (or other) entities that provide a degree of security and privacy. One of the reasons this can work is that the heirs don’t actually own the assets—The corporation (or other entity) does.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: ovide a degree of security and privacy. One of the reasons this can work is that the heirs don’t actually own the assets—The corporation (or other entity) does. www.proschoolonline.com Page 24: Contingency planning: Incapacity • Estate plans need to consider that the estate owner may become incapacitated • When an individual suffers from an incapacity, he or she has a disability—physical or mental—that impedes the ability to think and act as desired. Incapacity also can make a person legally ineligible to handle their own affairs. www.proschoolonline.com Mild cognitive impairment • Does not affect daily routine • Symptoms include occasional forgetfulness, disorientation, loss of focus, impulsiveness etc • Can progress to dementia Severe impairment • Can no longer function independently • Needs special care (24-hour home-care, else a care facility) Page 25: Addressing possibility of incapacity ➢ Part of planning for incapacity is ensuring a person’s will is updated, valid, and accurately expresses distribution intent along with specific directives, e.g., • Advance (care) directive.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: ty is ensuring a person’s will is updated, valid, and accurately expresses distribution intent along with specific directives, e.g., • Advance (care) directive. A broad category of documents identifying the individual’s desires for ongoing treatment when incapacitated. • Durable (enduring) power of attorney (health care proxy). authorizes a trusted individual to make health care decisions on the persons behalf, based on previously stated desires. • Health care/medical power of attorney. A medical POA grants specific authority for a representative to take control over health care decisions. • Springing power of attorney (conditional). A conditional power that springs into effect only in the event when the principal becomes incapacitated. • Living will. Provides direction to physicians regarding the level and degree of care • Revocable living trust. can provide a way to oversee legal and financial matters related to a person’s assets. However, this will only be true if a successor trustee is named.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: g trust. can provide a way to oversee legal and financial matters related to a person’s assets. However, this will only be true if a successor trustee is named. Page 26: Disclaiming Inheritance • There are times when an heir/beneficiary does not want to accept an inheritance and can disclaim or renounce the inheritance. • Disclaiming an inheritance is a formal (and legal) rejection of the property. • There may be many reasons, two of which are • Extreme inconvenience : Perhaps the heir already has enough assets and does not wish to increase his or her personal fortune. This is most likely to occur in cross-border situations, but also may be relevant within a territory. • Associated debt : When a person inherits an asset, often they also inherit any debt associated with that asset. Sometimes the debt can be quite large; large enough that the positive value of the asset is overshadowed by the debt. As a result, the beneficiary may decide not to accept the asset and thereby avoid inheriting the debt.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 1
chunks.text: positive value of the asset is overshadowed by the debt. As a result, the beneficiary may decide not to accept the asset and thereby avoid inheriting the debt. www.proschoolonline.com

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: Page 1: 3. Succession laws Page 2: International Laws: Common Law , Civil Law, Sharia Law www.proschoolonline.com Page 3: Forced Heirship (applies in certain countries) • Forced heirship means decedent is not free to distribute the estate as he or she desires. • There are strict rules to be followed . •The first, and largest share often going to the first-born (often male), then to the next in line, and so on. Children (protected heirs) almost always are provided for. •this may mean that the spouse effectively is disinherited or receives a nominal • Spain has a system of forced heirship, where typically, children are entitled to receive two- thirds of the deceased parent’s estate. The surviving spouse is entitled to at best, only the remaining one-third. •Three ways to circumvent this are as follows 1. Lifetime transfers. Assets that are gifted are no longer are part of the estate, therefore, at death, they are excluded in a forced heirship scheme. 2. Create a trust 3. Establish a foreign company to own property.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: er are part of the estate, therefore, at death, they are excluded in a forced heirship scheme. 2. Create a trust 3. Establish a foreign company to own property. www.proschoolonline.com Page 4: Forced Heirship- Usufruct • A number of forced heirship systems do provide a significant portion of the marital estate to be distributed to the surviving spouse, but the spousal portion may be reduced to provide for children. Often, the spouse’s share may be 50% of the estate, or at least the community property portion is provided to the spouse. Community property is marital property. • Usufruct technically refers to the ability of a person to use the property of another person, without changing the character of the property. It may be applied to use of land, buildings, and/or movable objects. • Usufruct is not specifically limited to surviving spouses, but it can be applied for their benefit. Usufruct can allow a surviving spouse to live in the family home, use community property assets, and the like. The right normally terminates at the earlier of remarriage or death.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: a surviving spouse to live in the family home, use community property assets, and the like. The right normally terminates at the earlier of remarriage or death. As such, usufruct is not necessarily as beneficial as outright inheritance and ownership, but it does provide a degree of security to the surviving spouse. www.proschoolonline.com Page 5: Indian laws: HSA 1956, Muslim Law, ISA 1925 • India is complex, multi-layered, diverse. • Succession planning in India is similarly complex, and depends on the personal laws of the deceased, which, in turn, is typically based on religion. • India has 3 separate laws relating to succession / estate www.proschoolonline.com Codified, applies to Hindus, Jains, Sikhs, Buddhists.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: lly based on religion. • India has 3 separate laws relating to succession / estate www.proschoolonline.com Codified, applies to Hindus, Jains, Sikhs, Buddhists. Uncodified, applies to all Muslims Codified, applies to Parsis, Christians, Jews or covered under Special Marriages Act, 1954 Page 6: Testate / Intestate planning as per Indian laws Person dies Muslim Muslim law Hindu, Sikh, Jain, Buddhist No will? HSA 1956 Will? ISA 1925 Christian, Parsi, Jew or SMA 1954 ISA 1925 Page 7: 1: HSA 1956 (Intestate only) • The Hindu Succession Act, 1965 (HSA), applies to intestate succession for • Hindus, Prarthana, Brahmo or Arya Samaj • Sikh or Buddhist • Excludes Christian, Muslim, Jew, Parsi and Hindus married under the Special Marriages Act, 1954. • It provides for 1. transfer of property with respect to two main schools of Hindu law, that is, Mitakshara and Dayabhaga, 2. classes of heirs, and devolution of property, disqualification, and other aspects relating to classes of heirs.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: Hindu law, that is, Mitakshara and Dayabhaga, 2. classes of heirs, and devolution of property, disqualification, and other aspects relating to classes of heirs. www.proschoolonline.com Page 8: Key concept: Class 1 to 4 heirs (Mitakshara) ➢ Mitakshara school uses principle of propinquity (i.e., nearest degree of blood relation) ➢ distributes property entirely to a single Class of heirs, i.e., to Class 1, else class 2 else 3 etc • Class 1 heirs: Sons, daughters, widow • Else Class 2 heirs: brothers and sisters of the deceased • Else Class 3 heirs: Agnates, i.e., related via male /father's side • Else Class 4 heirs: Cognates ➢ Additionally, • Children in womb are treated as heirs at time of death • Full-blood relations are preferred over half-blood / uterine-blood • In case of simultaneous death, the younger of the two deceased is assumed to have “survived” the elder Page 9: HSA 1956: Logic for sharing by Heir Class Class 1 • Property is tenants-in- common (not joint) • Widow(s) x 1 share (total) • Mother x 1 share • Sons or daughters x 1 share (each) • Deceased sons or daughters x 1 sh

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: perty is tenants-in- common (not joint) • Widow(s) x 1 share (total) • Mother x 1 share • Sons or daughters x 1 share (each) • Deceased sons or daughters x 1 share (each) • Shared between their widow, sons, daughter (son) or only sons, daughters (daughter) Class 2 • 9 Entries (i.e., sub- categories), each with a specified share • E.g., Entry 2 = Siblings of the deceased • All members within an Entry get equal parts Class 3 and 4 • Traced from male deceased up or down • Direct lineage from deceased is preferred to remote lineage • Within same lineage, closer generation to deceased is preferred over distant generation • If no preference can be found, heirs get equal shares Page 10: HSA 1956: Treatment of HUF ➢ HUF is considered a ‘person’ and is assessed as a separate legal entity (IT Act). • Coparcener is a person who acquires interest by birth, up to three generations from the senior most male member of the HUF. Coparceners can demand partition of the HUF.

Coparceners can demand partition of the HUF. • Karta: the senior most member of an HUF and tasked with the responsibility of managing the HUF and its property including the legal right to deal and alienate any HUF property, ➢ Property types • Ancestral Property: Acquired through inheritance from ancestors, always shared by coparceners equally. This type of property is also known as Coparcenary Property. • Joint Family Property: Acquired by the members of a HUF and includes ancestral property, wherein every coparcener has a joint interest in the property. • Self-acquired Property: Either joint family property that has now become separate, or property that is self-acquired by an individual Page 11: HSA 1956: Treatment of HUF Nature of Interest • All coparceners acquire interest by virtue of their birth in ancestral property Devolution on demise • Deceased’s share of ancestral and joint family property devolve upon the successors.

chunks.text: erest by virtue of their birth in ancestral property Devolution on demise • Deceased’s share of ancestral and joint family property devolve upon the successors. Alienation by Will • Post the HSA, a coparcener can now dispose of his interest in a coparcenary property by way of will. Alienation by Gift • Not allowed Page 12: Hindu Succession(Amendment) Act , 2005 ➢ The principal amendments mostly relate to provisions for females: • Coparcenary rights to Hindu females: Females are now equally entitled to a share in ancestral property as their male counterparts, and granted the same rights and liabilities • Abolition of survivorship: Interest of the deceased in property of a joint Hindu family will now devolve by testamentary or intestate succession. • Allotment of shares in coparcenary property to Hindu females: If a Hindu male (in a joint Hindu family governed by Mitakshara law) died after the 2005 Amendment, his daughter will be allotted the same share as his son. The share of a pre-deceased son or pre-deceased daughter will be allotted to their surviving child/children.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: aughter will be allotted the same share as his son. The share of a pre-deceased son or pre-deceased daughter will be allotted to their surviving child/children. • Abolition of pious obligation: Son, grandson or great grandson of a deceased no longer obliged to pay off all (legal or moral and illegal or immoral) debts of the deceased • Right of a female heir to claim partition: Hindu female heirs can claim partition of a dwelling house occupied by a joint family; earlier reserved only to Hindu male heirs. • A remarried widow of a pre-deceased son, pre-deceased son of a pre-deceased son or brother will inherit property of the intestate. Muslim Law (Shariat) • Mohammedan personal law is uncodified and based on the principles of Shariat, being the commandments of Allah. • It is multi-layered, highly nuanced, complex, and applies differently to different factions of Muslims. • Broadly, there are two schools of Mohammedan law 1. the Sunni laws, which relies on the Sunnah (record of the teachings of the Prophet), and is further subdivided into four schools i.e.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: hools of Mohammedan law 1. the Sunni laws, which relies on the Sunnah (record of the teachings of the Prophet), and is further subdivided into four schools i.e. Hanafi, Maliki, Shafii, and Hanbali. 2. the Shia laws, which relies on ayatollahs (i.e., religious leaders) interpretations of the Prophet's teachings, distinct from the Sunni school owing to interpretive differences.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2

The courts in India must abide by the Quran.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 2
chunks.text: ith legal principles, and of these 80 deal with the law of personal status, like inheritance, marriage and divorce. The courts in India must abide by the Quran. • The Sunna: Sunnah means “tradition” or “way” and is understood to be the way of life as deduced from the Prophet’s behaviors. E.g., aspects like voluntary charity, voluntary prayer and fasting, good table manners etc. • Ijma: Means consensus or agreement of Islamic scholars on a point of Islamic law. The rules set out by Ijma have varying degrees of binding authority in different schools. • Qiyas: the deduction of legal prescriptions from the Quran or Sunnah by analogic reasoning on matters not explicitly covered therein or unsystematic opinion is called Qiyas. It constitutes the fourth source of Muslim Law.

Indian Succession Act 1925 & property rules • Governs the succession laws applicable to • All Parsis, Christians, Jews • All persons married under the Special Marriages Act, 1954. • Only those Hindus, Jains, Sikhs, Buddhists who have a Will • Intestate death is subject to two legal concepts 1. Lex Situs (=where property is situated), which provides that any immovable property of a person who has died intestate will be regulated by the laws of India regardless of domicile at death. 2.

Lex Domicilli (= where deceased was domiciled), which provides that moveable property of a person who has died intestate will be regulated by the law of the country in which the deceased was domiciled at death. • Testamentary death considers provisions made in a Will www.proschoolonline.com Page 18: Key term: Domicile ➢ Domicile is defined as the country • that a person treats as their permanent home, (i.e., intention to stay) • or lives in and has a substantial connection with (i.e., physical presence) ➢ Domicile is important for cross border Estate ramifications, e.g., moveable property and a person must have a domicile from one of the below Domicile of Origin • Is the country where the father was domiciled at the time of birth of the child. • In the case of an illegitimate child, is the country where the mother was domiciled at the time of birth.

Domicile by Choice • Is the country where the person has a fixed habitation (i.e., not country of origin) • Onus is on the person to prove that they intend to continue residing in this country by choice Domicile by Law • is the domicile that is imposed by law • Origin or choice has no relevance in such cases Page 19: Key terms: Consanguinity, Generations ➢ Consanguinity or kindred relationship, per section 24, means “the connection or relation of persons descended from the same or common ancestor”. ➢ Consanguinity is of two types: Linear & Collateral Linear & Collateral consanguinity Generational consanguinity Page 20: Succession based on ISA • Important rules for determining succession: 1. there is no distinction between relatives on paternal and maternal sides (of the same degree). 2. no distinction is made between half blood and full blood relatives (of the same degree). 3.

Page 21: Intestate succession (ISA 1925) General rules • Survived by widow & heirs, then 1/3rd to widow, remaining shared equally between heirs • survived by the widow and kindred, then 50% to widow, 50% to kindred • no lineal descendants or kindred, then 100% to husband or wife Specific to Parsis • Normal rules like consanguinity do not apply • Lineal descendants who have died in the lifetime of the intestate without leaving a widow or any lineal descendant shall not be considered • A widow or widower of any relative of an intestate who has remarried during the lifetime of an intestate shall not be considered Page 22: Salient points of ISA 1925 ➢ Animus Testandi, or will signed by person of sound mind of their own free will ➢ Lapse of Legacy

Distribution vehicles Page 2: Key terms: Inheritance (what is received) ➢ Inheritance is the receipt of property from an estate, ➢ Ownership may be • Legal, i.e. right to manage the property, or dispose of it • Beneficial, i.e. right to use and enjoy the property ➢ Legal heirs have both kinds of ownership Execution of an Estate Leads to Inheritance Page 3: Key terms: Present Interest / Future Interest Present Interest • Allows immediate use, E.g. • inherits farmland and uses it immediately • Receives insurance immediately Future Interest • Cannot avail till a point in the future, E.g. • Receives house only after death • cannot access trust funds till testator dies ➢ When one receives / can use the inheritance is very important ➢ Inheritance can have Present or Future interest ➢ This governs when the benefit can be used by the recipient Page 4: Lifetime Transfers strategies • Lifetime, or inter vivos, transfers have two purposes, usually enabled via Gifting 1. removing assets from an estate to reduce tax and other concerns 2.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: egies • Lifetime, or inter vivos, transfers have two purposes, usually enabled via Gifting 1. removing assets from an estate to reduce tax and other concerns 2. see the recipient use the gift during ones lifetime • The recipient may be a family member, friend, partner, charity, alma mater, or other. • Regardless of intent, either the gift-giver or recipient may incur a tax 1. Gifts may be exempt, potentially exempt, or a fully taxable 2. Many territories have a lookback period for when the gift giver dies within a certain period of years following the transfer. 3. If a gift is potentially taxable, look at whether any reduction in value via appraisal, depreciation, or other, may be applied. www.proschoolonline.com Page 5: Gifts Page 6: Key term: Gifts ➢ A way to transfer assets during one’s lifetime ➢ Technically, a gift must be complete to qualify as a gift. • Complete means owner has relinquished all control over the property being transferred ➢ Minors cannot gift Gifts • Must be completed while doner is still alive • Owner can relinquish right (e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: hed all control over the property being transferred ➢ Minors cannot gift Gifts • Must be completed while doner is still alive • Owner can relinquish right (e.g. gift Rs 1 Cr) or transfer benefit (e.g. interest from Rs. 1 Cr) • Done so that doner can see the recipient enjoy the gift in their lifetime OR given to reduce the owners estate size for tax purposes (e.g. gifts 20 lacs from an estate of 1 Cr) Gift rules • Gift tax: Applies in some territories • Lookback period: Countries like UK will return gift value to estate if doner dies before lookback period is completed (e.g. within 7 years of gifting) Page 7: Gifts & gift taxation in India www.proschoolonline.com • If an immovable property above the value of INR 100 is to be gifted, the instrument of gift is required to be compulsorily registered with two witnesses • Mohammedan law sometimes permits an oral gift to be made of immovable property, known as ‘Hiba’.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: is required to be compulsorily registered with two witnesses • Mohammedan law sometimes permits an oral gift to be made of immovable property, known as ‘Hiba’. • Gifts are not taxable in India, provided they are from close relatives, which include grandparent and parents on both sides, spouse, siblings, children, grandchildren or spouses of any one of these • Gifts received from anyone not a close relative are taxable beyond Rs. 50,000 and must be declared as income in each Financial Year where aggregate value > 50,000 • Other then cash, Income Tax department will consider the value of the gift based on stamp duty value (for immovable property) or fair market price (for movable property). Page 8: Intra-Family Transfers (Loans) • Another method of making an intrafamily transfer is through use of a loan. • Used if the recipient does not want a gift • It allows assets to be kept within the family • To be a loan, a financial transfer must have a legally recognized agreement, a stated rate of interest and a repayment period with minimum required payment amounts.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: e a loan, a financial transfer must have a legally recognized agreement, a stated rate of interest and a repayment period with minimum required payment amounts. • An intrafamily loan is slightly different from a commercial loan, as usually • the interest rate charged can be quite a bit lower. The government may have regulations regarding how low the rate can be - it can be as low as 0% in some territories. • Repayment period can be extended. Additionally, loan repayment amounts stay in the family (e.g., from the child to the parent), and can be used to increase cash flow or whatever purpose the parent wants www.proschoolonline.com Page 9: How loans work 1. The parent cannot undervalue – e.g. a business worth $1 million has an intrafamily loan value of $100,000. The difference between the two values ($900,000) will be deemed a gift and treated accordingly. 2. loan effectively freezes the asset’s value as of the execution of the transfer. E.g., a transferred business is valued at $1 million and grows over time to be worth $10 million, the parents tax exposure is only $1 million. 3.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: the transfer. E.g., a transferred business is valued at $1 million and grows over time to be worth $10 million, the parents tax exposure is only $1 million. 3. On the negative side, a pitfall is allowing the loan to become a gift. • A parent may make a loan to a child and then forget about it. When this happens, the loan remains part of the parent’s estate and can be taxable. • A child may not honor the agreement. If the parent does not enforce the repayment requirement, the loan will be considered a gift and treated accordingly. 4. Care also should be exercised when considering the possibility that a married child gets divorced from his or her spouse. In the divorce decree, the property may be granted to the ex-spouse, and this almost never is what the parent desires. www.proschoolonline.com Page 10: Trusts Page 11: Key terms: Trusts • A way to transfer assets during one’s lifetime. • A Will can then be used to dispose of assets not included in the Trust.

chunks.text: 0: Trusts Page 11: Key terms: Trusts • A way to transfer assets during one’s lifetime. • A Will can then be used to dispose of assets not included in the Trust. • Trusts are transparent, flexible, able to meet special needs and tax efficient www.proschoolonline.com • The Indian Trusts Act, 1882 (ITA) governs private trusts. • It does not apply to Muslim Law, nor HUF property • Key participants in a trust are • Author /Settlor • Trustee (and Protector) • Beneficiary • Key terms are • Trust Deed • Trust Property Page 12: Types of Trusts in India Discretionary: When beneficiaries and/or their beneficial shares are not specified, and trustees can decide. Specific: When beneficiaries and/or their shares are fixed, and trustees have no say in this. Revocable/ Irrevocable: Pertains to whether the Trust can be modified once set up. Page 13: Offshore Trusts in India www.proschoolonline.com • An offshore trust, in the Indian context, is a trust formed outside India by an Indian resident.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: up. Page 13: Offshore Trusts in India www.proschoolonline.com • An offshore trust, in the Indian context, is a trust formed outside India by an Indian resident. It allows an Indian resident to • hold, own, transfer or invest in foreign currency, security and immovable property outside India • if such assets were owned, held or acquired by him while he was a resident outside India • or if he has inherited property from a person who was resident outside India. • If an Indian resident wishes to transfer funds to an offshore trust, he may do so through the Liberalized Remittance Scheme (LRS), under which upto USD 250,000 per person per financial year may be remitted overseas without RBI permission. • The Reserve Bank of India (RBI), through the Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 (FEMA) and Exchange Control Regulations oversees the parties to a trust on the basis of residential status.

There is no ‘one size fits all’ in a family arrangement or settlement, each family having its own objectives. 1. memorandum or agreement which either sets out the acts, deeds, matters and things to be completed, or which itself operates as an instrument of transfer of any or all assets 2.

It’s a catch-all statement that keeps anything from falling into a place where the probate court will have to determine the distribution.

chunks.text: erson or organization. It’s a catch-all statement that keeps anything from falling into a place where the probate court will have to determine the distribution. www.proschoolonline.com Page 21: Classification of Wills (India) Privileged Will (Section 65) • A special will made in extraordinary circumstances like war or a dangerous expedition. • Such will can be made oral or in writing. • Section 65 provides that any will made by a soldier or an airman or a mariner, who has attained the age of eighteen, when he is in actual service and is engaged in actual warfare, would be privileged. • Such person can dispose of his property by a will as prescribed by the mode, manner and rules as described in section 66 Unprivileged Will (Section 63) • Every other person can only make an unprivileged will (i.e., does not fall into privileged wills) • Unprivileged wills include joint wills, mutual wills and contingent wills. • must be in writing, contain a signature or mark of the testator in presence of two attesting witnesses who must also sign or affix their marks in the presence of the testator.

Page 22: Types of Wills (India) Contingent Will • bestowment of estate is subject to fulfilment of condition or event mentioned in the will. • It is executed only if the conditions are met, failing which the will is void. Concurrent Will • two wills written by one person, E.g., one dealing with immovable and the other movable property. • They co-exist with one another and collectively are held to be valid in law. Mutual Will • executed by two persons mutually who confer reciprocal benefits on each other, E.g., husband and wife • The terms of such will are binding on the surviving testator after the death of the other. Joint Will • two or more persons agree to make a conjoint will to determine how their joint estate will devolve upon their beneficiaries.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: he death of the other. Joint Will • two or more persons agree to make a conjoint will to determine how their joint estate will devolve upon their beneficiaries. • Becomes irrevocable on death of any one of the testators Page 23: Drafting a Will www.proschoolonline.com Page 24: Will requirements (as per ISA 1925) • Conditions for a good Will 1. be clear and describe, in detail, the estate, and contain unambiguous bequests, and provide for alternate bequests in case primary bequests fail or are invalid; 2. contain a residuary provision to cover any part of the estate that is not dealt with specifically; 3. although not mandatory, it is advisable to name an executor 4. an optional ‘In-Terrorem’ provision stating if a beneficiary challenges the will or bequests, they will lose all benefits under the will. While this person can legally challenge a will, the ‘in-terrorem’ is an efficient deterrent. ➢ A will may be declared void if it is: 1. made by a person of unsound mind or a minor 2. obtained by fraud, coercion, or importunity 3.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: an efficient deterrent. ➢ A will may be declared void if it is: 1. made by a person of unsound mind or a minor 2. obtained by fraud, coercion, or importunity 3. a privileged will and the testator survives beyond a month from making it. 4. not expressive of any definite intention (void for uncertainty). Page 25: Bequests in a Will • Types of bequest in a Will • Specific Bequest: a specific asset or assets, e.g., movable, immovable • Percentage Bequest: a specific percentage of your overall estate • Residuary Bequest: the remaining balance of the estate after all specific bequests • Contingent Bequest: takes effect if heir(s) reject the bequest or do not survive the testator. • Onerous Bequest: legatee can either accept in totality or reject in totality Page 26: Wills: Attestation, Registration (ISA 1925) ➢ A Will must be attested to by two witnesses in the presence of the testator ➢ Registration: A will’s genuineness cannot be challenged on grounds of non-registration. • Nevertheless, registration of a Wills have some advantages 1.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: stator ➢ Registration: A will’s genuineness cannot be challenged on grounds of non-registration. • Nevertheless, registration of a Wills have some advantages 1. Safety: kept in the safe custody in the office of the Registrar 2. Security: access restricted as per Section 57 of Registration Act Page 27: Amendments and Codicils (ISA 1925) • the testator is always entitled to alter and/or revoke a will, and has three options 1. overwrite the original will and obtain new signatures of the attesting witnesses, 2. preparing a codicil: A codicil is an instrument specifically made in relation to an executed will, that explains, alters or adds to its dispositions, and is deemed to form part of the will. In practice if will is registered, codicil should also be registered. 3. preparing and executing a new will.

Advised when • structural changes are needed for a will, • or when a will requires an overall change • or the circumstances warrant changing substantial dispositions or bequests, • or has several codicils that can create administrative confusion Page 28: In summary: Trusts v/s Wills www.proschoolonline.com Page 29: Types of Estates ➢ Gross Estate is everything owned by the decedent. • All current assets, investment accounts, portion of jointly held assets, amounts owed to the individual, but not yet paid, ongoing royalties, real estate rents, retirement accounts, life insurance, revocable living trust assets, trust assets where the decedent was the beneficiary, real property, and business interests. ➢ Probate estate, only those assets that pass through probate. • Only those assets distributed according to a will or by the rules of intestacy.

It does not include life insurance, irrevocable trust assets, certain ownership titles or any other assets that pass according to law. ➢ Taxable estate will vary by territory. • Taxable Estate = Gross estate less the following • Less assets legislatively carved out of the taxable estate (e.g. assets going to spouse) • Less debts and other expenses (last rites, liquidity, medical bills, admin costs etc) • Less any taxable exemptions (e.g. how an asset is owned or titled) Page 30: 5. Legal aspects Page 31: Transfer at Death –Probate Process • Probate is the orderly process of estate distribution according to a valid will or applicable statute (e.g., rules of intestacy). • It is handled through a probate court (which may or may not hold that title, depending on the territory). It is orderly , fair, structured • A disinterested, unbiased third party will have made a ruling that is as fair as possible.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: itle, depending on the territory). It is orderly , fair, structured • A disinterested, unbiased third party will have made a ruling that is as fair as possible. • Probate provides a venue for heirs to receive valid titles and other forms showing a change in ownership. It also gives creditors a venue to ensure legally valid claims are honoured. • Downsides : Expensive, time delay and information can be made public (Famous people concern) www.proschoolonline.com Page 32: Duties of an Executor www.proschoolonline.com 1. Aggregate and assessed value of Estate 2. Honor specific and proportionate general legacies in the manner prescribed by the ISA 1925 3. Provide funds for the performance of funeral ceremonies of the deceased, if estate has such funds available 4. Collect the property of the deceased and the debts that were due to him at the time he was living, with reasonable and due diligence. 5. Preparing and filing tax returns and paying taxes out of the estate. This may require the executor to liquidate assets.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: g, with reasonable and due diligence. 5. Preparing and filing tax returns and paying taxes out of the estate. This may require the executor to liquidate assets. Page 33: Powers of the Executor (India) An Executor has been granted the same powers as the deceased. 1. Can sue any party for all causes of action or to recover debts owed. 2. Can prosecute or defend any parties 3. Can spend money out of the deceased’s estate to care for property belonging to the estate 4. Has complete legal right over the assets of a testator and can dispose of and deal with the property of the deceased in such manner as he may think fit. However, there are certain caveats or exceptions to this power : • limited by any restriction contained in the will, unless Probate overrules these restrictions • may not, without the permission of the court – • mortgage, charge or transfer by sale, gift, exchange or otherwise any immoveable property for the time being vested in him under section 211; or • lease any property for a term exceeding five years. www.proschoolonline.com Page 34: Powers of the Executor 5.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: time being vested in him under section 211; or • lease any property for a term exceeding five years. www.proschoolonline.com Page 34: Powers of the Executor 5. Cannot purchase any part of the property of the deceased, either directly or indirectly (to ensure fiduciary objectivity) 6. If a Will names several executors or administrators, they will enjoy the same powers 7.Same powers are given to a minor appointed as an administrator as if he/she was appointed as an administrator who has attained the age of majority. 8. Section 315 gives a married woman to whom a probate or letters of administration has been granted, the same powers as an ordinary executor or administrator. www.proschoolonline.com Page 35: Obtaining a Probate ➢ Obtaining a Probate : A probate is granted by a competent court of law. Different states have different procedures to applying for and obtaining probate.

chunks.title: Cfp Estate Lecture 3
chunks.text: bate ➢ Obtaining a Probate : A probate is granted by a competent court of law. Different states have different procedures to applying for and obtaining probate. Typically, a petition for probate must contain • Schedule of properties, assets, credits and debts of the deceased • documents such as the executor’s oath, affidavits of consent of legal heirs and at least one attesting witness. • value of properties movable and immovable to ascertain the net value in Indian Rupees (INR). ➢ The court will then issue a notice to invite claims by creditors and heirs and also verify that the executor is the person that is authorized in the Will ➢ Finally, the court will issue a Succession Certificate, specifying its ruling on distribution ➢ Applicability of a Probate : A probate once granted acts as formal permission to the executor to transfer the properties to whom they are bequeathed. The executor is provided with a certificate proving the Will it is genuine.

Secondly, expenses of obtaining probate and costs that may be incurred in respect of judicial proceedings necessary to administer estate, are to be paid 3. thirdly, wages due to any labourer, artisan or domestic servant, for services rendered within three months of death, are to be paid. 4. fourthly, other debts according to their respective priorities 5.

Finally, the executor can then petition for permission to distribute what is left of the decedent's assets to the beneficiaries named in the will • No creditor shall have a right of priority over another; but the executor or administrator shall pay all such debts as he knows of, including his own, equally and in proportion as far as the assets of the deceased will extend.

chunks.text: Page 1: CFA www.proschoolonline.com ASSET CLASSES AND RETURNS Page 2: Equity Page 3: Equity (common stock) How shares become Equity (common stocks): IPO • Privately held companies (e.g., start-ups) have shares that are split between owners or partners • When this company wants to raise capital from the public it will approach an investment bank to underwrite the offering (IPO) • As the underwriter, the investment bank can choose 1 of 2 paths: 1. Best Efforts Commitment - simply facilitates the sale to the public. 2. Firm Commitment Underwriting – it buys all shares to resell them. • IPO requires the company to also • develop and deliver offer documentation, including a prospectus. • use the services of other entities like accounting and law firms, ( collectively, the selling syndicate) • Post IPO, these shares are traded on the Stock Exchange as common stocks Page 4: • Equity are traded in four types of securities markets • Retail investors are typically only involved with the first two.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: ock Exchange as common stocks Page 4: • Equity are traded in four types of securities markets • Retail investors are typically only involved with the first two. www.proschoolonline.com Ways to buy/sell Common Stock PRIMARY MARKET • Where all initial public offerings (or IPOs) occur. • These are stocks and bonds that are being offered to the public for the first time. • When buying in this market, the investor is buying the security directly from the issuer. SECONDARY MARKET • The secondary market is where investors trade existing securities. • Transactions in this market normally happen through one of the stock exchanges • In these transactions, the seller of the securities, rather than the issuing company, receives the proceeds. THIRD MARKET • The third market is used by institutional investors and broker-dealers who are not exchange members, to trade large blocks of exchange-listed securities outside of exchanges, known as the “OTC” or “over-the- counter” market. FOURTH MARKET • The fourth market is also for institutional investors.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: listed securities outside of exchanges, known as the “OTC” or “over-the- counter” market. FOURTH MARKET • The fourth market is also for institutional investors. • Here, institutions buy and sell securities directly with one another through private computer networks. • INSTINET (Institutional Networks Corporation), owned by Nomura Holdings in Tokyo, is an example of one of these networks. Can be fixed / variable coupon (e.g., London Interbank Offered Rate -LIBOR) • Par value: Issued at par value and traded (bought and sold) in the over-the-counter market or exchanges • Higher quality / Lower quality / Junk bonds: Terms relating to the risk (& associated return) of a bond • Trading at discount / Premium: When the coupon rate is lower / higher than current rates on new bonds • Terms of issue, includes the bond’s coupon rate, maturity date, and other conditions that may be relevant. • Call provision allows the bond issuer to “call in” and redeem the bond at a predetermined price • Call premium: When the call price is higher than the face value, the difference is referred to as the call premium. • Call protection: Bond is said to have protection in the years between the issue of a bond and its call date.

Duration (risk) • Measures the interest rate risk of fixed income securities • Used by investors who intend to sell before maturity • E.g., if interest rates rises 1%, a bond with a duration of six would decrease in value about 6%. Yield Curve • shows whether it is better to hold short-, medium- or long- term bonds • Positive curve: Pays better in Long term • Negative curve: Pays better in short term • Flat: no difference Page 17: www.proschoolonline.com • Preferred stock generally pays a fixed dividend, thereby providing a fixed income to the investor.

It has an annual dividend of $5 per share and her required rate of return is 5%.

Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) www.proschoolonline.com ➢ An ETF will track one index and replicate its returns, e.g. gold, stock, commodity etc ➢ ETF’s can be constructed in two ways • Include every security in the index, a strategy known as full replication.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: its returns, e.g. gold, stock, commodity etc ➢ ETF’s can be constructed in two ways • Include every security in the index, a strategy known as full replication. • Include a representative sample of the index, called sampling replication Advantages • Cost Efficiency: ETFs have significantly lower expenses than mutual funds • Tax Efficiency: ETF owners control the timing of their purchases and sales • Availability of Option Contracts Disadvantages • NAV and Tracking Error: Do not always trade exactly at their net asset value, leading to performance that differs from the index. • The primary cause of tracking error in an ETF relates to how it is constructed ( replicant / sampling as above) Page 23: 3. Exchange-Traded Notes (ETNs) www.proschoolonline.com ➢ ETN’s are loan instrument issued by a financial entity, such as a bank. They do not have any underlying securities as they are linked to a particular stock index. ➢ It comes with a set maturity period, usually from 10 to 30 years.

They do not have any underlying securities as they are linked to a particular stock index. ➢ It comes with a set maturity period, usually from 10 to 30 years. ➢ ETNs are linked to a stock index, thus do not possess any voting rights, nor pay a fixed rate of interest. ➢ The gains or losses of the investor are derived from the performance of an asset class or index that it tracks. ➢ ETNs are most commonly used to track commodity and currency exchange markets, where mutual funds and ETFs aren’t typically available. ➢ ETNs are tied to the creditworthiness of the issuer. This influence investors to buy. Page 24: 4. Seller must sell these shares if exercised. • Buyer can, but is under no obligation, to sell the underlying assets • Seller is obliged to buy the underlying, if exercised. • E.g., Buyer has option to sell 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 40. Seller must buy these shares if exercise.

Page 32: How Options work Call Buyers Expects price to rise, so they can make a profit by buying asset below market price Sellers Expect price to fall, so they can profit from the option premium (since buyer will not exercise) Put Buyers Expect price to fall, so they can make a profit by selling asset above market price Sellers Expect price to rise, so that they make a profit from the option premium (since buyer will not exercise) • Options can be on any asset, e.g., equity, debt, currency, etc Page 33: SWAPS • Swaps are option that typically swap a fixed- interest income stream from one party for a floating rate from another party. • The floating rate payments are typically based on the London Interbank Offered Rate, or LIBOR. • Banks often initiate swaps and offer them to corporations, another bank or investors. • The types of swap are interest rate swap, currency swap, commodity swap and credit default swap.

However, they both are used in risk analysis. ➢ Covariance shows us how the two variables vary, i.e. co-variance ➢ Correlation shows us the relationship, i.e. co-related ➢ They are dependent on each other as Covariance = Correlation x SD1 x SD2 Covariance Correlation Meaning Covariance is an indicator of how two random variables are dependent on each other. A higher number denotes higher dependency. Correlation indicates how strongly these two variables are related, provided other conditions are constant. The maximum value is +1, representing a perfect dependent relationship. Inter-relationship Covariance is used to deduce correlation a measure of covariance on a standard scale. It is deduced by dividing the calculated covariance by standard deviation. Expected values The value of covariance lies in the range of -∞ and +∞. Page 51: Systematic risk: Coefficient of Determination (R2) www.proschoolonline.com • Systematic risk is undiversifiable • It is valuable to be able to determine how much of a portfolio’s (or asset’s) risk is undiversifiable. • This measure is called the coefficient of determination, R2, or R-squared. • Formula: The correlation coefficient is squared, i.e. if R =0.3, then R2 is 0.9 • As an example, correlation coefficient between Portfolio A and the FTSE 100 is 0.30. Squaring the correlation coefficient, the result is 0.090. • This means that 9.00% of Portfolio A’s price movement is directly associated to the movement of the FTSE 100 (i.e., systematic risk). Page 52: Systematic risk: Beta www.proschoolonline.com • Beta is also used to measure systematic risk. • If an asset or portfolio has a beta of 1.00 against its benchmark(s), and the market goes up 10.00%, the asset can be expected to also rise 10.00%.

There are no taxes or transaction costs Some agents can form well-diversified portfolios All securities have finite expected values and variances Assumptions Page 77: Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) www.proschoolonline.com • Fama, in his Ph.D. dissertation called EMH, stated that at any given time and in a liquid market, security prices fully reflect all available information • EMH could be divided into three forms: Weak • Assumes current price reflects all information • Then past price and volume data of a stock have no impact on the future direction of the stock’s price. • Then technical analysis is not beneficial, since it adds no predictive value.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: ume data of a stock have no impact on the future direction of the stock’s price. • Then technical analysis is not beneficial, since it adds no predictive value. Semi strong • Assumes new information is immediately factored into stock prices by investors and the market regains equilibrium • the semi-strong form effectively discounts fundamental analysis. Strong • Assumes current stock prices fully reflect all information, including insider information (i.e., a perfect market), and is available to all investors • Therefore, one cannot use such information to consistently achieve excess returns. Page 78: Random Walk www.proschoolonline.com • Certain implications arise from acceptance of efficient market hypothesis. • One of those is that the past movement or direction of the price of a stock cannot be used to predict its future movement • That future stock prices evolve according to a random walk and thus cannot be predicted with accuracy. • This is the focus of the random walk hypothesis.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: ment • That future stock prices evolve according to a random walk and thus cannot be predicted with accuracy. • This is the focus of the random walk hypothesis. • If random walk is completely valid, one has no reason to do either fundamental or technical analysis. Example – Selling & buying to rebalance Example – SIP with no rebalancing Page 88: Active and Passive Management • We looked at Jensen’s alpha, and how to determine the degree to which an asset manager is adding value to the portfolio. • The consideration of alpha brings us to the next portfolio decision: active or passive management.

E.g. Warren Buffett, Peter Lynch, Sir John Templeton. Peter Lynch’s suggestions are: 1. Keep return expectations realistic (neither too high nor too low). 2. If the best you can do is match market performance, buy index funds. 3.

Adapt the portfolio to changing client requirements, as necessary. • Passive management generates lower fees than active management Page 90: www.proschoolonline.com 1- LADDER • A bond ladder is a portfolio of individual bonds with staggered maturities, for eg: The first bond could mature in one year, the second in two years, the third in three years, and so on. • As each bond matures, the money is used to purchase a new bond with a five-year maturity. • Using this process, interest rates would be locked in for each five-year period, but the entire amount is never subject to long-term interest rate (reinvestment) risk. • Laddering also helps maintain liquidity, because on maturity amount becomes available each year (rather than the entire amount being locked up for the full period until maturity).

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: s maintain liquidity, because on maturity amount becomes available each year (rather than the entire amount being locked up for the full period until maturity). 2- BULLET • A bullet portfolio holds almost all its bonds in intermediate maturities, rather than spreading the maturities out. • Investors may hold a small amount of short-term (1-3 year) bonds and long-term bonds (20 years), but the majority will be intermediate term (10 years). • An investor or advisor opting for a bullet portfolio is effectively “betting” that during the target maturity window interest rates on offer for reinvestment will be relatively attractive. Pure Fixed Income Securities Page 91: www.proschoolonline.com 3- IMMUNIZATION • Bond immunization is an investment strategy used to minimize the interest rate risk of bond investments by adjusting the portfolio duration to match the investor’s investment time horizon. • The immunization process helps to protect the portfolio against decreases to principal because of interest rate increases.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: investor’s investment time horizon. • The immunization process helps to protect the portfolio against decreases to principal because of interest rate increases. • For example: If the client wants to retire in 10 years, bond portfolio duration could be adjusted to match the 10-year time frame. This would help immunize the portfolio against interest rate and reinvestment rate risk. 4- BARBELL • Barbells purchase short-term and long- term bonds (but no mid-term bonds). • The idea is to lock in higher interest rates with longer-term bonds, while the short-maturity bonds provide flexibility and liquidity. • If rates fall, when they mature, the short-term bonds can be invested in other assets with higher returns.

Discounted Cash Flows www.proschoolonline.com • The DCF methodology consists of 6 steps: 1. forecast the discretionary cash flow that a company is expected to generate in each of the next few years, generally three to five years. 2. discount to a present value amount using a discount rate 3. calculate terminal value by estimating the annual discretionary cash flow beyond the forecast period, divided by a capitalization rate. 4. Discount the terminal value to a present value amount using the same discount rate applied to the discretionary cash flows 5.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4
chunks.text: vided by a capitalization rate. 4. Discount the terminal value to a present value amount using the same discount rate applied to the discretionary cash flows 5. Add the present value of the terminal values to the present value of the discretionary cash flows to calculate the enterprise value of the company 6. Interest bearing debt is deducted from enterprise value to derive the shareholder value of the company. Page 106: 3. Page 110: Behavioral Finance www.proschoolonline.com Page 111: Introduction Behavioural Finance • Science of applying psychology to finance. • Disputes the notion that markets are efficient and rational. • Suggests that Human Beings are not always rational and do not always act in a rational manner . • The impact of this on financial markets, and more specifically, on individual investors is significant. www.proschoolonline.com Page 112: Investor Biases, Effect of Money, and guidance 1. Research has shown that people are affected by internal biases, resulting from hereditary or experiential reasons. • 21 investor biases exist in three categories: 1. general behavioral biases (6), 2. information processing biases (8) and 3.

Hence, an advisor needs to apply behavioural finance techniques to better manage client and their personality www.proschoolonline.com Page 113: Behavioural biases Cognitive Dissonance: • When newly acquired information conflicts with a pre-existing notion or understanding, people will often perform rationalization to maintain psychological stability. Conservatism: • People cling to prior views or forecasts at the expense of acknowledging new information • Results in specific denial of, or under-reaction to new information Confirmation: • Refers to a type of selective perception that emphasizes ideas that confirm our beliefs, while devaluing whatever might contradictthem. Representativeness: • People often apply prior life experiences to new situations.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Global Lec 1 4

Sometimes this works, but other times it may lead to making significant financial errors. Illusion of Control: • T endency to believe that one can control or influence outcomes, when this is not possible in many circumstances. Hindsight: • People tend to overestimate the accuracy of their own predictions and underestimate actual levels of uncertainty. Page 114: Information processing biases Anchoring and Adjustment: • rather than deciding based on pure value, we factor in comparative value Mental Accounting: • Tendency of grouping assets into non-interchangeable mental accounts. Framing: • Tendency to respond to various situations differently based on the context. Availability: • people estimate the probability of a new outcome based on its familiarity in their lives. Ambiguity Aversion: • People tend to dislike uncertainty (or ambiguity) more than they dislike risk.

Outcome: • Deciding based on the outcomes of events, without researching about the steps leading to it Recency: • causes an individual to place more emphasis on recent events than those that occurred in the past. Page 115: Emotional biases Loss Aversion: • Person places more emphasis on recent events than those that occurred in the past. A participant can simultaneously play all three roles i.e. 1. can buy shares of one company with objective of making a long-term investment, generally in which case he is called as an investor (his action = investment); 2.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: can buy shares of one company with objective of making a long-term investment, generally in which case he is called as an investor (his action = investment); 2. trade in the stock with a view to gain from the volatility in the short term, in which case he is called a speculator (his action = speculation); and 3. trade in the same stock in two different markets and benefit from the momentary price differences, in which case he is called an arbitrageur (his action = arbitraging). A high promoter stake is generally positive, as it shows promoter confidence. • An increase in promoter shareholding is an indication of the increased confidence of the promoter in the business growth. • A decrease in promoter holding must be studied carefully but not immediately looked upon as a negative for the business or the company. • a well-diversified shareholding pattern indicates promoter freedom is somewhat constrained. Page 30: 5. Equity Derivatives Market www.proschoolonline.com • Derivatives are based on underlying assets such as equity, bonds, commodities, currencies, metals, gold, index’s etc.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: rivatives Market www.proschoolonline.com • Derivatives are based on underlying assets such as equity, bonds, commodities, currencies, metals, gold, index’s etc. • In 2000, SEBI permitted the exchanges BSE & NSE to launch equity derivatives • RBI is responsible for regulating all interest rate, forex, and credit derivatives, including OTC derivatives. • Nevertheless, there is a large unregulated OTC market in India • Exchange cash flows / instruments • Buy / sell at set price • Buy/sell asset later • Customised for future delivery Forward Futures SwapsOptions Page 31: Equity Derivatives: OTC Market www.proschoolonline.com The OTC derivatives markets have the following features compared to exchange traded derivatives. • Counterparty (credit) risk is decentralized and located within individual institution.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: ts have the following features compared to exchange traded derivatives. • Counterparty (credit) risk is decentralized and located within individual institution. • There are no formal centralized limits on individual positions, leverage, or margining • There are no formal rules for risk and burden-sharing • There are no formal rules for ensuring market stability and safeguarding the collective interests of market participants, • The OTC contracts are not regulated by a regulatory authority and the exchange's self- regulatory organization, although they are affected indirectly by national legal systems, banking supervision and market surveillance. Some of the features of OTC derivatives markets embody risks to financial market stability. i. the dynamic nature of gross credit exposures ii. information asymmetries iii. the effects of OTC derivative activities on available aggregate credit iv. the high concentration of OTC derivative activities in major institutions; and v. the central role of OTC derivatives markets in the global financial system.

Seller lost 250, so has to top up 250 = Mark to Market Future pricing 1. Cost of carry method = Spot price + (Carry cost – Carry return) 2. Expectancy method: Based on expectation of spot and future prices Page 33: Derivatives: Options www.proschoolonline.com Index options (have index as underlying). Option Premium = Intrinsic Value + Time Value (& paid by buyer) Only In the money options have intrinsic value. On- or Out have 0 intrinsic value. Intrinsic Value of a Call = S-X Intrinsic Value of a Put = X-S Value of an Option calculated using either Binomial Pricing Model or Black-Scholes Model Hedgers, Arbitrageurs, Speculators Page 34: Index futures vs Index Options vs Index Funds www.proschoolonline.com INDEX FUTURES • Index futures have a future level or index and an expiry date (settlement date). • While dealing in index futures, margins are paid, per traded lot size basis, to the exchange by the seller as well as the buyer to enter a futures contract.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: . • While dealing in index futures, margins are paid, per traded lot size basis, to the exchange by the seller as well as the buyer to enter a futures contract. • Index futures have ample liquidity with much lower trading costs • They have marked-to-market mechanism whereby an individual would be required to bring in more cash if the margins fall short on day-to-day basis due to adverse direction of the market than the intended one. INDEX OPTIONS • Index options are another form of derivatives which give the buyer of option the right, but not the obligation • Like index futures, index options can also be used to either bet on the directional movement of markets or hedging market exposures • Like index futures, index options are also exchange settled in cash.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: be used to either bet on the directional movement of markets or hedging market exposures • Like index futures, index options are also exchange settled in cash. • The costs in index futures are less than index options as the options premium cost a little more for the buyer at a reduced risk INDEX FUNDS/ ETF’s • Offered by MF’s • The subsequent levels of index fund price are represented by the net asset value (NAV) which closely corresponds to the underlying index level (rise/fall) • While an index fund can be bought or sold at the closing level of index on a certain trade date, ETFs can be traded at intra-day levels. • The risk managed is fund manager risk, both on the up and down sides, while the market risk also applies on index funds and ETFs. The nomination form cannot be signed by Power of attorney (POA) holders. Every new nomination for a folio/account will overwrite the existing nomination. The nomination shall be mandatory for new folios/accounts opened by individuals especially with sole holding and no new folios/accounts for individuals in single holding should be opened without nomination. Even those investors who do not wish to nominate must sign separately confirming their non-intention to nominate. Delisting of Shares Delisting of shares refers to the permanent removal of the shares of a company from being listed on a stock exchange. Delisting may be compulsory or voluntary. NSDL and CDSL in turn have depository participants (DP), who are banks, stockbrokers, NBFC’s, registrar and transfer agents, etc. DPs maintain investor holdings, related to both depositories, NSDL and CDSL All equity shares, bonds and debentures, as well as derivative products are held by DPs on behalf of investors in electronic form. DPs receive requests from investors to dematerialize securities held in physical form. The reverse of the process is called re- materialization. This can be one of three options Existing benchmark • E.g., Sensex or ETF • Goal is to beat this benchmark • This meets generic characteristics of a good benchmark, i.e., free of conflict, transparent, objective pricing etc Customised benchmark • Advisor will construct a benchmark based on client objectives and restrictions • It should reflect the advisor’s investment style, e.g., focus on large and mid-cap etc • Else it is unrealistic Managers Universe benchmark • Consists of a group of managers with similar investment styles • Goal is to beat this benchmark • Is inherently flawed as it only includes managers with good performance, thus does not represent average performance Page 88: Active management: Performance attribution www.proschoolonline.com • Performance attribution evaluates three aspects of an actively managed portfolio Allocation • Evaluates if asset/sector allocation had a positive or negative contribution • Pos
