Page 89: Address few critical risks to Investing in Equities Transactional Risk and Liquidity Risk www.proschoolonline.com Transactional Risk • Transactional risk can be best explained by way of exchange rate fluctuations. It is the difference in price due to the change in exchange rate between the time the contract was entered into and its eventual settlement. • An individual living in the United States wants to add 100 shares that are listed on the London stock exchange to his portfolio. This transaction will therefore be in pounds. • If the price per share is £10 at the time of sale and the exchange rate at the time of sale is $1/£0.72, the exchange value for the cost of share purchase equivalent to £1000 pounds is $1389. However, payment does not occur at time of purchase. When payment is made the exchange rate fluctuates which results in a depreciation of the dollar to $1/£0.65 pounds, resulting in a cost of $1,538.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: of purchase. When payment is made the exchange rate fluctuates which results in a depreciation of the dollar to $1/£0.65 pounds, resulting in a cost of $1,538. Liquidity Risk • Liquidity risk arises when an investor is not able to sell shares quickly enough without a loss of capital or reduction in price. Buyers will not buy shares that they think will not make a profit. • To find buyers and entice them back prices will need to drop, and this could lead to a correction in prices. Liquidity risk can also be considered specific to a company. • The risk for the company is that the company may not be able to meet its short-term debt obligations. • This could cause investors to sell shares increasing supply and a potential drop in share price. • The company could go into bankruptcy which will result in a capital loss for the investor. Page 93: Active management: Equity analysis (DCF) www.proschoolonline.com Discounted cash flow model is used to determine the intrinsic value of stock by discounting the value of future cash flows to a present value. The DCF methodology consists of 6 steps Forecast the discretionary cash flow Calculate the terminal value Discount terminal value to PV, using same discount rate as step 1 Add terminal value PV to discretionary cash flow to find Enterprise value Adjust enterprise value Calculate the output’s sensitivity Page 94: Active management: Equity analysis (Other models) There are two other commonly used absolute valuation models, the residual income model, and the asset-based model. 1. Residual income model focuses on probability and values a business as the book value per share plus the PV of future residual income.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: the asset-based model. 1. Residual income model focuses on probability and values a business as the book value per share plus the PV of future residual income. Residual income in equity valuation is the net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) generated by a company less the companies’ cost of capital. This model is like the dividend discount model where instead of dividends residual income is used. This model is useful when valuing a company that does not pay dividends and if the forecasted FCFE is expected to be negative. It is the cash available to shareholders after considering of the costs of all a company’s capital. • Residual income = NOPAT – (C% × TC) • NOPAT is the company’s net operating profit after taxes, C% is the cost of capital, and TC is total capital. 2. Asset-based model is based on assets minus liabilities and looks at what the business could sell for in the current market based on net assets.

chunks.title: Cfp Ipam Indian Mkts
chunks.text: otal capital. 2. Asset-based model is based on assets minus liabilities and looks at what the business could sell for in the current market based on net assets. The value of assets and liabilities will differ from the value reported on the balance sheet and the market value as factors such as timing and accounting for depreciation will impact the values. Another differentiating factor is net assets adjustments for assets that may not be on the balance sheet such as intangible assets as well as adjusting liabilities. www.proschoolonline.com Page 95: Active management: Equity analysis (Relative valuation) It is advised that a combination of absolute valuation methods (DCF etc) and relative valuation measures be used while taking equity decisions. chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: Page 1: CFA www.proschoolonline.com Risk Management & Insurance Key theory concepts Page 2: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com What are Financial risks? • Risks can be of two types 1. Low-level daily risks: E.g., Catching a cold 2. Substantive future risks: E.g., House getting robbed, being in a car accident • Risks are shown as a probability between 0% (never occur) and 100% (guaranteed) Financial risk is the possibility of Financial loss Risks that are high probability, e.g. • Age-related health issues • Demise at some point in time Risks that are low probability, e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: sibility of Financial loss Risks that are high probability, e.g. • Age-related health issues • Demise at some point in time Risks that are low probability, e.g. • House damaged due to freak weather Page 3: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Types of financial risk Speculative • Risks that can generate either profit or loss (or no change) • Insurance will not cover such risks • Examples include • Gambling • Starting a business • Investing in stocks Pure • Risks that only have potential for loss • No possibility of profit or gain • Insurance coverage is often available for pure risks • Examples include • Loss of income • Death or illness • Property damage etc Page 4: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Risk → Perils  Hazards ➢Insurance policies specify a list of Perils that they will cover ➢Perils are the cause of losses, e.g. • Hail and fire cause property damage • Cancer can lead to disability or death.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: cify a list of Perils that they will cover ➢Perils are the cause of losses, e.g. • Hail and fire cause property damage • Cancer can lead to disability or death. ➢Hazards increase chance of Peril • Physical hazards like road conditions, driving under influence etc • Moral hazards like faking death to claim insurance • Morale hazards (forgetfulness or carelessness) ➢Examples • Property damage (=Substantive future risk) • Perils (=Specified in Policy) • Fire, hail, lightening • Theft, robbery • Hazards: (= increased chance of peril) • Lack of fire safety in building • High crime rate in locality Page 5: CFA Risk Management Process Page 6: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com What is risk management / Process of risk management Process of addressing concerns relating to insurable and uninsurable risks Identify risk management goals Collect information to identify risk exposures facing the client Analyze and evaluate the information Construct a risk management strategy (i.e., determine appropriate risk treatment methods) Implement the recommendations Monitor the recommendations

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: information Construct a risk management strategy (i.e., determine appropriate risk treatment methods) Implement the recommendations Monitor the recommendations for any needed changes Page 7: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Risk management techniques • For each aspect of life, you can develop a risk strategy • For e.g., for a person who actively treks in the mountains • Reduce: Wear proper safety gear • Avoid: Stop trekking.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: of life, you can develop a risk strategy • For e.g., for a person who actively treks in the mountains • Reduce: Wear proper safety gear • Avoid: Stop trekking. Hence no risk • Retain: Take the risk that an accident may happen while trekking • Transfer: Buy insurance for possible accidents while trekking Reduce Avoid Retain Transfer High Severity Low Severity High chance Low chance Page 8: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com 3 rules when in doubt about recommending Insurance • In cases when a risk management decision is not obvious, check if any of the following apply to the situation and act accordingly Consider the odds • Higher the odds of a risk occurring, higher its premium • Insuring a high- probability risk will be exorbitant Don’t risk a lot for a little • Don’t carry a catastrophic loss if the cost to insure is small Don’t risk more than you can lose • Don’t be greedy. • Take calculated risks that will not ruin you if they fail.

Insurable interest • Insurable interest is the basis of all insurance policies. • To pass this check, there needs to be an object and an associated financial hardship • I.e. the insured suffers financial hardship if a defined object occurs • To exercise insurable interest, the policyholder must buy insurance on the item or entity (the insured interest). • Any change in the insurable interest usually requires a new policy. Company insures its CEO • Object = Life of CEO • Financial hardship = cost of rehiring and retraining Flat-owner insures own flat • Object = Damage to flat from perils • Financial hardship = catastrophic loss Individual buys health insurance • Object = Health care for self • Financial hardship = cost of healthcare Page 14: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com 2. Indemnity • Indemnity means to make whole i.e. restore condition to what it was prior to loss • It restricts the Policy-holder to the amount of covered loss, i.e.

chunks.text: demnity • Indemnity means to make whole i.e. restore condition to what it was prior to loss • It restricts the Policy-holder to the amount of covered loss, i.e. it ensures a policy- holder does not make a loss or a profit from a policy • In effect, Indemnity ensure a policy-holder is not able to claim more than their loss • Indemnity does not strictly apply to Life or Personal Accident insurance, since they are classified as “contingent” risks House insured for Rs 50 lacs • Event: Fire • Insurance received: Rs 50 lacs • The fact that the current market price of house was Rs 80 lacs does not matter Gold insured for Rs2000 • Event: Theft • Was valued 3 years ago • Since then gold has appreciated 100% however insurer still pays Rs 2000 Life insured • Event: Death • Unable to make whole Page 15: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com 3.

E.g. • The insurer pays its client’s claim for losses directly, then seeks reimbursement from the other party's insurance company. • By law, the Insurer can also sue the party in order to recover due money • It also restricts the insurer from making a profit on the claim Auto insurance • Insurer pays the car owner for damage to car • Insurer claims this amount from the insurer who’s policy owner caused the damage Health insurance • If a policy-holder is injured in a car accident, the Insurer can recover the claim from the party at fault Special case • Occasionally a policy can have Subrogation waived by the Policy-holder • In such case, the Insurer will raise the premium as they cannot subrogate Page 17: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Additional contract terms and sections • Aleatory: Financial participation is unequal • Adhesion: Insurer made it, so must abide by terms • Unilateral: one-directional from Insurer t

chunks.text: oschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Underinsurance / Co-insurance Underinsurance (= problem) • Arises when insured value is lower than actual value • E.g. home valued at 100,000 by insurer but insured for a value of 70,000 by homeowner • Becomes a problem when all policy-holders do the same thing • Since the insurer then receives insufficient premiums to maintain viability of the pool Coinsurance ( = solution), applied in case of Underinsurance • Clause that states that only complete destruction will result in payment of sum insured • In all other cases, coinsurance (or average) formula will apply for partial damage • (Actual Amount of Insurance ) * Amount of Loss = Amount of Claim (Required Amount) = 70000/100000 or 70% of loss amount will be paid under Coinsurance clause Page 19: CFA www.proschoolonline.com Insurance in India Page 20: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Governments support Insurance as a sector 1.

Attract investments & FDI growth: By deregulating the sector (allowing private players to participate) and opening to Foreign Direct Investment in Insurance companies 3. Individual safety nets: On an individual basis, insurance money provides support to the family in the event of loss due to death, immobility, work restraints, illness etc. 4.

Stimulate society: Due to external risks being managed through insurance, society can focus on productive activities like buying homes or expanding businesses and demand Benefits of Insurance to the Government ➢ A well-built insurance sector provides the Government with a safety net from loss-causing events and enhances its risk-taking ability Page 21: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Indian laws relating to Insurance Laws help - Protect the customer - Regulate the insurance companies Acts The Insurance Act, 1938 The Insurance Laws (Amendment) Act, 2015 Indian Contract Act, 1872 Consumer Protection Act, 2015 Doctrines of Waiver and Equitable Estoppel Page 22: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com The Insurance Act, 1938 The Insurance Act, 1938 was the primary legislation governing all types of insurance to furnish strict state jurisdiction over insurance business (Amended in 2002 applicable to whole o

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: was the primary legislation governing all types of insurance to furnish strict state jurisdiction over insurance business (Amended in 2002 applicable to whole of India). Part I of this Act lays down the definitions such as ‘the insurance company’, ‘insurer’, ‘insurance agent’, ‘actuary’, ‘intermediaries’ and others related to all aspects of the Insurance industry. Part II is the main section of this Act which deals with the provisions applicable to the insurers. Part V of this Act lays down the penalty for default in complying with this Act. It also gives power to the Authority (IRDA) to make regulations. Page 23: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com The Insurance Laws (Amendments) Act, 2015 ➢ This Act amended three previous Acts related to the insurance sector in India: - The Insurance Act, 1938. - The General Insurance Business (Nationalization) Act, 1972. - The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority Act, 1999.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: India: - The Insurance Act, 1938. - The General Insurance Business (Nationalization) Act, 1972. - The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority Act, 1999. ➢ Under the new Act: - Provisions related to investments by insurance companies and prohibition for investment of funds outside India have been strengthened. - The power of investigation and inspection by the Authority has been further increased. - Provisions related to assignment and transfer of insurance policies, nomination of policy holder, prohibition of payment by way of commission have all been strengthened. - Revised limitation of management expenses in insurance business have been set. - New provisions have been inserted related to the appointment and record of insurance agents. ➢ As per Section 7A, the cap on Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) in Indian insurance companies has been raised to 49% providing additional capital flow in the insurance sector. ➢ To set up an efficient grievance redressal system, the Securities Appellate Tribunal (SAT) has been made the appellate authority to IRDAI’s order.

Page 24: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Indian Contract Act, 1872 ➢ Section 182 of the Act defines an “agent” as a person employed to do any act for another, or to represent another in dealings with third persons, known as the “principal”. ➢ It is important for a financial advisor to understand the laws regarding Agency because nearly all business transactions worldwide are meted out through this relationship between the Agent and the Principal. ➢ An Agency is created when one person delegates his authority to some different person, or appoints him to do some specific tasks in their specified areas of work. ➢ It also deals with appointment and authority of agents/sub-agents, Agent’s duty to Principal and Principal’s duty to Agent amongst other important subject matters.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: also deals with appointment and authority of agents/sub-agents, Agent’s duty to Principal and Principal’s duty to Agent amongst other important subject matters. Page 25: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Consumer Protection Act, 2019 ➢ The Consumer Protection Act, 2019 is enacted to protect the interests of the consumers in India. It makes provision for the establishment of authorities for timely and effective administration and settlement of consumer’s disputes. It applies to all goods and services. ➢ The 2019 Act repeals the 1986 Act whereby it has substantially enhanced the scope of protection afforded to consumers by bringing within its purview advertising claims, endorsements and product liability. ➢ While it continues to have Dispute Redressal Commissions at the District, State and National levels, the monetary value of complaints that can be entertained at each of these commissions has been increased substantially. ➢ The 2019 Act also introduces the power of judicial review, which will allow Consumer Commissions to review their orders.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: ons has been increased substantially. ➢ The 2019 Act also introduces the power of judicial review, which will allow Consumer Commissions to review their orders. ➢ The IRDA act along with Consumer Protection Act, 1986 helps protects the interest of consumers and give them the right to seek consumer redressal. It would provide consumers rights to prevent consumers from fraud and unfair practices while availing insurance services Page 26: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Doctrines of Waiver & Equitable Estoppel ➢ Waiver is the conscious and discretionary giving up of a known legal right. ➢ A waiver can be express or implied. ➢ Express waiver could be oral or written. In either case, it is a clear statement that a right is being given up. ➢ Implied waivers are created through the conduct of the waiving party. E.g.,, if the insurance company accepts the premium from a policyholder after the expiration of the grace period, the company has impliedly waived its right to claim that the policy had lapsed.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: ccepts the premium from a policyholder after the expiration of the grace period, the company has impliedly waived its right to claim that the policy had lapsed. ➢ The legal doctrines of Waiver and Estoppels are directly related to the responsibilities of insurance agents. They are important to individuals because they can work to their advantage when dealing with an insurance company. ➢ Estoppel: “a legal rule that prevents someone from changing their mind about something they have previously said is true in court”. ➢ Often described as a rule of fair play. ➢ From the insurance point of view, an insurer may waive a right, and after the policy holder has relied upon the waiver and acted upon it, the insurer will be stopped from claiming the right. ➢ Example: An individual fails to disclose some information in the insurance proposal form and the insurer does not request that information and issues the insurance policy (a waiver). In future when a claim arises, the insurer cannot question the contract because of non-disclosure. This is estoppel.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: and issues the insurance policy (a waiver). In future when a claim arises, the insurer cannot question the contract because of non-disclosure. This is estoppel. Page 27: CFA www.proschoolonline.com Regulatory Infrastructure around Insurance Page 28: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com Structure of Indian Insurance ➢ two categories – Life and General ➢ both governed by the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) • IRDAI established via Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority (IRDAI) Act, 1999. • Indian postal department provides life insurance through Postal Life Insurance • This is exempt from the purview of IRDAI ➢ As of 2019, 70 insurance companies active • 24 are life insurance (including LIC ) • 27 more are general insurance • 7 are stand-alone health • 12 are re-insurers Page 29: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.com 1. About IRDAI ➢ The Insurance Act, 1938 provides the powers to IRDAI ➢ IRDAI is an autonomous and statutory body incorporated in 2000 following recommendations of the Malhotra Committee.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 1
chunks.text: nce Act, 1938 provides the powers to IRDAI ➢ IRDAI is an autonomous and statutory body incorporated in 2000 following recommendations of the Malhotra Committee. ➢ It has a 10-member board, appointed by the government (Chairman, 5 full- time directors, 4 part-time directors) ➢ Its Mission as stated in the preamble to the IRDAI Act, 1999, is “to protect the interests of holders of insurance policies, to regulate, promote and ensure orderly growth of the Insurance industry” ➢ Its goals are 1. Encourage systematic growth of the industry. 2. Protect the interest of the policyholders. 3. Promote high standards of integrity in the market 4. Speed up claim settlement. 5. Set standards and conduct vigilance.

Life Insurance Council: Assists the life insurers in maintaining high standards of service to policyholders. General Insurance Council: Promotes better understanding of non-life insurance industry. Aims to strengthen the non-life insurance industries. chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: Page 1: CF A www.proschoolonline.com Life Insurance Page 2: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Life Insurance: Context 1. Term • Is for a term E.g., 20 years • Original form of insurance • Used to cover risk during working years 2. Traditional • Provide whole life benefit regardless of when one dies • Used as means of providing an inheritance or a source of cash flow 3. Non-traditional • Allow more flexibility and unbundling • Allows tax- deferred investment opportunities (akin to mutual funds) 4. Joint-life • Cover more than one insurer • Used for business buy-sell or for estate planning of couples • Is unique amongst Insurance policies in that every policy will pay out if in force • Risk is taken by both parties • Insurer risk is pay-out within a short while of policy issuance • Policy-holder risk is outliving the policy or live a long time and pay premiums Page 3: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m 1.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: of policy issuance • Policy-holder risk is outliving the policy or live a long time and pay premiums Page 3: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m 1. Term Insurance • Fixed Term: Used to cover a period (term) when loss of life is a risk • Typically, till one reaches 60 or 70 years • Post this age, premiums become exorbitant (ref image) • Post this age, Term Insurance is rarely renewed, as • reached retirement age and thus no loss in income • or child has started working so parent’s risk of support income has passed • Decreasing term: Used to cover outstanding debt (e.g., home loans or similar) • Premiums are fixed but less than than Fixed Term • Has a decreasing death benefit that roughly matches amortization schedule of loan Page 4: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m 2. Traditional Permanent policies • Insurance Co. creates a cash value from premiums that can generates conservative returns • Three types of traditional policies are available Whole Life • Protects for whole life • Endow at a specific age, e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: that can generates conservative returns • Three types of traditional policies are available Whole Life • Protects for whole life • Endow at a specific age, e.g. 100 or older • Initial premiums are much higher and are converted to cash value after policy expenses are paid Limited / Modified Life 1.Limited Life are like Whole life, but with fewer, larger premiums 2.Modified Life combines term and whole life. • Used to cover children (term) then switches to whole. Endowment • On completion, the Policy holder will receive cash proceeds equal to death value • These are used to be popular as savings vehicles, but have become less popular nowadays Page 5: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m 3.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: eath value • These are used to be popular as savings vehicles, but have become less popular nowadays Page 5: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m 3. Non-Traditional Permanent policies • Allow more flexibility than traditional policies as well as unbundling • Provide access to tax-deferred investment opportunities (akin to mutual funds) Universal Life • Flexible / adjustable premiums, that can be skipped occasionally if cash value is sufficient • Unbundles mortality charges, expense charges, interest rates Variable Life • Are fixed and bundled • Allows the policy- holder to select high- return investment vehicles for cash value • At the cost of foregoing the cash value guarantee Variable Universal • Flexible + unbundled • Combines the flexible premium approach with the option to invest in high-return vehicles • Guarantees only mortality charges Page 6: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m 4.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: emium approach with the option to invest in high-return vehicles • Guarantees only mortality charges Page 6: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m 4. Joint Life • Cover more than one insurer • Can be structured as either whole life or universal life policies First to die policy • Covers 2 or more individuals • Pays death benefit when the first person covered dies • Suitable for business buy-sell agreements • Cheaper than two single policies Second to die policy • Covers two people • Pays death benefit on death of the second person (survivorship policy) • Suitable for Estate planning of a couple on 2nd death • Cheaper than two single policies Page 7: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key concepts of individual life insurance • Entire contract clause states entire policy along with attached application constitutes the contract and any changes must be in writing / signed by the company (waiver clause) • Ownership rights three entities (policyholder, insured, beneficiary) of which the policy- holder has all policy rights.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: signed by the company (waiver clause) • Ownership rights three entities (policyholder, insured, beneficiary) of which the policy- holder has all policy rights. Owner can assign rights (Absolute = All, Collateral = Some) to another person, e.g., Collateral to a bank as security for a loan • Grace period typically 30 or 31 days from due date of premium. If paid within this period, policy will not lapse. In case of death during this period, death benefit is adjusted. • Contestable period The initial 1 or 2 years during which an insurer can contest death proceeds arising from misstatement or misrepresentation (i.e., disclosures). Thereafter insurer cannot deny a claim on these grounds, except in case of blatant fraud. • Misstatement of age should the insurer misstate his or her age, the insurer will adjust the death benefit accordingly based on premium provided for the correct age.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: tatement of age should the insurer misstate his or her age, the insurer will adjust the death benefit accordingly based on premium provided for the correct age. Page 8: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key concepts of individual life insurance • Reinstatement allows policy to be reinstated on clearing of past dues and proof of insurability any time post lapse to even up to 7 years in some cases. • Nonforfeiture options Cash value polices have this for situations where policyholder no longer wants to pay premiums. A nonforfeiture table provided by Insurer shows amounts available for each policy year that can be used to adjust policy • Policy loan allows policy-holder to borrow against Cash Value. Unpaid loans or interest are deducted from proceeds on death, or if large enough, can cause policy to lapse with tax implications. Interest rates charged by the Insurer similar to any loan.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: deducted from proceeds on death, or if large enough, can cause policy to lapse with tax implications. Interest rates charged by the Insurer similar to any loan. • Suicide/ Aviation / War exclusions special exclusions for Suicide (if within 2 years of policy start, only premiums with interest are returnable), Aviation accidents (historically excluded in all cases) and War (military and civilian death are excluded during war times). Page 9: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key concepts of individual life insurance • Beneficiary the entity ( or entities) designated to receive the proceeds. A policy can and should have Primary as well as Contingent beneficiaries (for instances when Primary is dead or unwilling to accept proceeds). Most cases a beneficiary can be revoked by policy owner, however an Irrevocable beneficiary is also possible (e.g. ex-spouse) • Beneficiary payments can be made basis either the per capita or per stirpes rule and case should be taken to prevent undesirable distribution.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: e.g. ex-spouse) • Beneficiary payments can be made basis either the per capita or per stirpes rule and case should be taken to prevent undesirable distribution. • For e.g., if beneficiaries are 2 primary + 2 children and 1 primary dies before Insured • Case 1: per capita rule will divide equally. I.e. Surviving primary + children get 33% each • Case 2: per stirpes rule (by line of descent) will divide by class, i.e. 50% to surviving primary and remaining 50% split between the 2 children of the deceased • Conversion clause allows the policyholder to convert the term insurance into a cash value. This is usually done to convert term into a more suitable option. Clauses relating to types of cash value as well as period or insurer age apply. Page 10: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key concepts of individual life insurance • Dividends some policies pay dividends and are called participating. These arise when the Insurance company has a surplus profit.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: of individual life insurance • Dividends some policies pay dividends and are called participating. These arise when the Insurance company has a surplus profit. The Board declares a dividend, which can be availed by the policyholder in 5 ways (cash, left to accumulate, offset against premium payments, purchase paid-up additions or purchase 1 year term insurance • Common disaster states that should insured and sole primary beneficiary die together, proceeds directly and automatically move to secondary beneficiary • Settlement options cash is the most common way of setting a policy. Alternatives are covered under the Annuity chapter • Riders are marketing aids used by the Insurer and work like endorsements. Examples include • Return of premium on survival, Inclusion of spouse or children, Waiver of premium on disability, Accidental death multiplier, Early benefit for terminal or critical illness (pre-death) Page 11: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Income based method: Human Life Value (HLV) • Estimate future average annual earnings • E.g.

chunks.text: re-death) Page 11: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Income based method: Human Life Value (HLV) • Estimate future average annual earnings • E.g. 50,000Step 1 • Subtract taxes, premiums and living expenses (= PMT) • E.g. 50,000 – 20,000 = 30,000Step 2 • Estimate number of earning years till retirement (n) • E.g. n = 25Step 3 • Determine appropriate discount rate (I/Yr) • E.g. 4%Step 4 • Calculate PV of payment stream as an annuity due (PVAD) • PVAD = 487,409Step 5 Accounts for working life of client, but limited to fixed expenses, doesn’t factor uncertainty Page 12: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Needs based method (L.I.F.E.) • Determine if Life insurance is needed to pay off debt • E.g.

chunks.text: certainty Page 12: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Needs based method (L.I.F.E.) • Determine if Life insurance is needed to pay off debt • E.g. Mortgage, Education loan, Car loan, Credit card balancesLiability • Determine if insurance is a replacement for Income • Income can be calculated via HLV method or Retention method Income replacement • Provide liquidity to cover cost of last rites, bequests, emergency expenses on death etcFinal expenses • Like income replacement, estimate future cost of education • Estimate future cost of college then discount to PV Education funding Personalized need-based approach – more thought but takes longer to compute Page 13: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Annuities • The flip-side of Life insurance. Protect against risk of living too long • Insurance companies offer Annuities, since it also is based on mortality factors About annuities • A contract from an Insurance Co. • Buyer deposits money and Insurance Co.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: mpanies offer Annuities, since it also is based on mortality factors About annuities • A contract from an Insurance Co. • Buyer deposits money and Insurance Co. returns a stream of payments • Goal is to spread invested capital and earned interest over the life of the annuitant (i.e. receiver) • Actuaries and mortality factors are used to determine amount of each periodic payment (based on age and sometime gender etc) Types of Annuities 1.Immediate: Payments within 1 month 2.Deferred: Payments at a future date 3.Fixed: Fixed and level payments 4.Variable: Depend on market returns 5.Indexed: Usually fixed + variable (market index) Page 14: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Ways of measuring index for Indexed Annuities • Participation rate: Sets the % that is linked to the stock market. • E.g. 90% participation rate means that if the stock market increased by 10% in a index interval (e.g. 1, 5, 7 or even 10 years) then the annuity account value would increase by 9% in that interval (i.e.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: the stock market increased by 10% in a index interval (e.g. 1, 5, 7 or even 10 years) then the annuity account value would increase by 9% in that interval (i.e. 90%*10%) • Change in the market is measured in a number of ways • Percentage change: Difference in end and beginning of Index Interval. Ignores all points between. • Ratchet method / Point to Point: Locks in the gain on an end of year basis • Spread method: Subtracts a fixed % (usually 2 or 3%) from the percentage change over an Index Interval. E.g. if % change over 3 years is 30%, then 28% is credited. • Downside protection • If market falls in an index interval, the account remains unchanged from its start point. i.e. the annuity owner did not lose any money due to market loss Page 15: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key concepts of Annuities • Irrevocable once payments begin, the annuity becomes irrevocable • Inflation adjusted even a 4% inflation halves purchasing power in just 18 years.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: Annuities • Irrevocable once payments begin, the annuity becomes irrevocable • Inflation adjusted even a 4% inflation halves purchasing power in just 18 years. One should look for inflation-adjusted annuity schemes to cover this risk • Premium payments are the terms governing how the money is deposited into the annuity. Can be single, fixed or flexible schedules. • Annuitize the start of a steady flow of payments made to the beneficiary from the accumulated annuity contract. No further deposits can be made to annuity. • Payments the series of payments made to the owner after the contract is executed (immediate) or at some point in the future (deferred) • Single/Joint/ Joint and last survivor annuity payments made to a single person / payments made till death of first person / payments made till death of 2nd person Page 16: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key concepts of Annuities • Settlement and Pay outs • Period certain annuity payments continue for a minimum number of years.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: nline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key concepts of Annuities • Settlement and Pay outs • Period certain annuity payments continue for a minimum number of years. If the annuitant precedes the period certain, remaining funds go to the beneficiary • Lump sum most common form of settlement chosen by beneficiaries • Lifetime income beneficiaries spread settlement over a period. However, on their death, payments stop irrespective of money remaining in account. There is no recourse • Single payments made to a single person • Joint made to two people till death of first person • Joint and last survivor annuity payments till death of 2nd person.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: ngle payments made to a single person • Joint made to two people till death of first person • Joint and last survivor annuity payments till death of 2nd person. May remain level for both or reduced to 0.5 on death of primary annuitant unless survivor option is there Page 17: CF A www.proschoolonline.com LIFE INSURANCE IN INDIA Page 18: Life insurance evolution in India www.proschoolonline.com 1818 • Oriental Life established in Kolkata 1870 • Enactment of British insurance Act 1871-97 • 3 more companies start (Bombay Mutual, Oriental, Empire) 1912 • 1st statutory measure (Indian Life assurance companies Act) 1928 • Indian Insurance Company Act enacted (to allow government to collect insured data) 1938 • Insurance Act 1950 • Amendment act (got rid of Principal Agencies) 1956 • Nationalization of Life Insurance, LIC formed and absorbs 154 Indian, 16 non-Indian and 75 provident societies 1993 • Recommendation committee set up (under R.N.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: ationalization of Life Insurance, LIC formed and absorbs 154 Indian, 16 non-Indian and 75 provident societies 1993 • Recommendation committee set up (under R.N. Malhotra) 1999 • IRDAI constituted based on committee recommendations 2000 • IRDAI opens market to FDI Page 19: Income Sources for Insurance companies ⮚Insurance companies mostly generate revenue in three ways: www.proschoolonline.com Income from underwriting (=Premiums) Income from investing premiums Unpaid claims on lapsed policies • Premiums are fixed based on risk of each policy • Premiums are collected over time and aggregate • Claims are received and settled out of premiums • Net revenue (i.e. total premiums less total claims paid) = Underwriting Income • Policies are usually long term (e.g., 10-15 years) • Premiums thus invested in long term instruments • There are Low-risk instruments (i.e., low risk low return instruments) • Interest from these investments = Investment income • Lapses occur when people either stop paying premiums (i.e. abandon) or the policy expires with no claim.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: • Interest from these investments = Investment income • Lapses occur when people either stop paying premiums (i.e. abandon) or the policy expires with no claim. • Total premium collected against this policy = Lapsed policy income Page 20: Premium and its components ⮚Premium has three associated terms www.proschoolonline.com Pure (or net) premium Load Gross rate • Premium that is calculated based on actuarial studies • Is the amount needed to pay for losses and loss- related expenses • Premium that is meant to cover internal expenses of the insurer • Is the amount needed to pay for the Insurer’s sales effort, overheads, profit margin etc. • Premium that is finally charged to the policy applicant • Gross premium = Gross rate x number of exposed units Page 21: How Insurance Companies fix the gross premium rate Rate fixing (also called insurance pricing) is the determination of what rates, or premiums, is to be charged. It is also the process of establishing rates for reinsurance or other risk transfer mechanisms.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: the determination of what rates, or premiums, is to be charged. It is also the process of establishing rates for reinsurance or other risk transfer mechanisms. www.proschoolonline.com Life insurance actuaries determine the probability of death in any given year Based on this probability, expected value of the loss payment is determined Expected future payments are discounted back to the start of the coverage period Summation of all the Present values of future payment is used to determine the Net Single Premium Net single premium is converted to premium instalments Loading for expenses is added to determine the gross premiums Page 22: Factors in fixation of premium & Rate making www.proschoolonline.com ⮚ The process of premium fixation involves various considerations including selling goals, competition, legal restrictions and estimating future costs related to the transfer of risk. Such costs include the amount of claims and expenses of their settlement, operational and administrative expenses, and the cost of capital.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: he transfer of risk. Such costs include the amount of claims and expenses of their settlement, operational and administrative expenses, and the cost of capital. ⮚ Insurers face a problem in setting fair and adequate premiums. Since the premium pays for insurance coverage in the immediate future, actual losses and expenses are not known when the premium is collected. Factors in determining the rate 1.Mortality Table 2. Guaranteed Benefits 3. Adverse Selection 4. Age, Medical condition Page 23: Factor 1: Mortality 1. MORTALITY TABLES AND ACTUARIAL VALUATION www.proschoolonline.com ⮚ A mortality table (life table or actuarial table) shows the rate of deaths likely to occur in a defined population during a selected time interval. ⮚ It shows the probability of a person's death before his next birthday, based on his current age. ⮚ Thus, a mortality table gives a probability of the number of people who are expected to die per 1,000 living in any given year. ⮚ They typically cover from birth of a person through his age 100, in one-year increments.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: of people who are expected to die per 1,000 living in any given year. ⮚ They typically cover from birth of a person through his age 100, in one-year increments. Page 24: Factor 2: Age & health • Younger people have lower premiums (less chance of dying young, likelihood of paying many premiums over their life)1. Age • Insurers determine the current health state of the individual2. Current Health Status • History of serious illnesses increase the premium 3. Medical History • Higher premium for larger sum assured, or shorter terms. 4. Death Benefit & Term • Males have higher premium (in general) than women of same age5. Gender • Higher premiums for smoking or drinking6. Smoking & Drinking • Dangerous occupation (e.g., shipping, mining, fishing) have higher premiums 7. Occupation Page 25: Factor 3 and 4 3. RATES OF GUARANTEED BENEFITS ⮚ Insurer often provide a Guaranteed Benefit plans for policy holders to attract risk adverse investors with Life insurance + guaranteed pay-outs ⮚ In a traditional policy, the Bonus is variable, whereas a Guaranteed policy offers a Fixed rate of return.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: investors with Life insurance + guaranteed pay-outs ⮚ In a traditional policy, the Bonus is variable, whereas a Guaranteed policy offers a Fixed rate of return. ⮚ The plans that come with such options usually charge a higher premium than the other non-guaranteed plans. ⮚ Therefore, the returns after adjusting for the costs on account of the guarantee, are low in such plans. ⮚ The higher the return promised, more shall be the premium charged & vice verse 4. ADVERSE SELECTION ⮚ ‘Adverse selection’ refers to insurance firm's acceptance of applicants who have concealed information about their actual condition. ⮚ In such situations, the insurer, with less information, is at a disadvantage to the applicant in determining the correct premium. ⮚ As the risk is not factored at the time of sale of the policy, this adverse selection leads to faulty determination of premiums and loss to the insurance company. ⮚ Truth is, the insurance company cannot possibly know every potential buyers health profile.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: aulty determination of premiums and loss to the insurance company. ⮚ Truth is, the insurance company cannot possibly know every potential buyers health profile. Therefore, before the sale, the company enforces detailed questionnaires followed by a very detailed underwriting process. Page 26: Distribution of Benefits ⮚ All the premiums collected from the policyholders get accumulated in the corpus of the insurance company. A large part of this is invested by the company in government secured debt instruments and a small part is invested in equities. ⮚ Based on earnings from these investments, valuation of the assets and liabilities of the insurance company, and the claims paid, the insurance company distributes the profits to ‘with-profits’ policyholders. This is termed a bonus.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: iabilities of the insurance company, and the claims paid, the insurance company distributes the profits to ‘with-profits’ policyholders. This is termed a bonus. www.proschoolonline.com Types of Bonuses Simple Reversionary Bonus Compound Reversionary Bonus Cash Bonus Interim Bonus Loyalty Bonus Terminal/ Final/ Persistency Bonus Survival Bonus Types of Bonuses in an Insurance Policy Page 27: Loans Eligibility Against Life Insurance Policies Insurance companies place conditions for disbursing the loan, some of which are: www.proschoolonline.com 1. Only available against traditional life insurance policies, as the guaranteed policy return is used as a collateral. 2. A three-year waiting period before approving a loan, to check if policyholder has been regular in paying premiums or not. 3. The loan amount is 80% to 90% of the surrender value (traditional plans) or the corpus value at the time of application (ULIP) 4. The interest rates are generally lower than those charged on personal loans 5. The policy holder must continue paying the premiums even after availing the loan. 6.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: interest rates are generally lower than those charged on personal loans 5. The policy holder must continue paying the premiums even after availing the loan. 6. The policy is assigned to the insurance company as a security till the loan is repaid. Page 28: Group Insurance Scheme Who Pays the Premium? ⮚ Some companies absorb the entire cost of the policy as a benefit to its employees. Others collected by the employer, it can be a deduction from the salary where the contribution may either be full premium or partial premium. ⮚ A person normally remains covered if he continues to work for a certain employer, unlike in an individual insurance policy where the insurer often has the right to reject the policyholder’s policy renewal, depending on the risk profile. Group Term Insurance Scheme ⮚ Provides coverage to a large group of people under a single policy. ⮚ Owner of the policy is the employer / organization, and policy covers all the members / employees of the organization. ⮚ It provides financial security to the dependent families in the absence of the employee.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: n, and policy covers all the members / employees of the organization. ⮚ It provides financial security to the dependent families in the absence of the employee. Although the employees may have their own individual insurance plans, yet a group term insurance policy is beneficial overall. ⮚ Several group insurance schemes additionally offer covers for outstanding loans to borrowers, while others offer benefits with critical illness and disability. Such a policy does not entitle the employee to any proceeds (sum assured) upon surviving the term but provides the payout of lump sum proceeds in the event of demise. Page 29: Employees’ Deposit Linked Insurance (EDLI) Scheme Who Pays the Premium? ⮚ Some companies absorb the entire cost of the policy as a benefit to its employees. ⮚ Others collect a portion from the employee. This is usually deducted from the salary EDLI Scheme ⮚ Employees can purchase this scheme to cover all the current members / employees of the organization.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: yee. This is usually deducted from the salary EDLI Scheme ⮚ Employees can purchase this scheme to cover all the current members / employees of the organization. ⮚ In the case of death of an employee, it provides a lumsum to the nominated depended (i.e., spouse, unmarried daughter or a male child up to 25 years of age). The exact amount depends on the last 12 months salary. ⮚ Several EDLI schemes also offer cover for outstanding loans to employees and benefits for critical illness and disability. ⮚ EDLI schemes do not provide any proceeds (sum assured) upon surviving the term. ⮚ Policy covers employees only during their service. It cannot be renewed by an individual after exit. Payout calculation ▪ 30 x Average of last 12 month’s salary of the Employee (capped at Rs. 15,000 p.m.) + Bonus Amount (Rs. 2,50,000). ▪ Therefore, the maximum payout under EDLI is 30 x 15,000 + 250,000 = 700,000 ⮚ Form 5 IF must be filled by the nominee after the death of the member and signed and certified by the employer. Page 30: Employees’ Deposit Linked Insurance (EDLI) Scheme 1.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: t be filled by the nominee after the death of the member and signed and certified by the employer. Page 30: Employees’ Deposit Linked Insurance (EDLI) Scheme 1. all active members of Employee Provident Fund are automatically covered 2. There is no exclusion for any member 3. There is no minimum service required 4. age, education, gender etc. don’t affect eligibility 6. Employer contributes 0.5% of monthly salary up to a maximum of Rs. 75 per month. No fee is deducted from the employee’s salary. 7. Effective 15th February 2018, the payout is between Rs. 2,50,000 and Rs. 7,00,000. 8. The maximum average monthly salary is capped at Rs. 15,000. 9. Average monthly salary is calculated as Basic + Dearness Allowance. 10. Covers the person irrespective of them being in India or abroad. 11. Benefits are transferable with any job change to the new employer 12. Pay out to nominees, family members or the legal heirs.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: them being in India or abroad. 11. Benefits are transferable with any job change to the new employer 12. Pay out to nominees, family members or the legal heirs. www.proschoolonline.com SALIENT FEATURES OF THE SCHEME ARE: Page 31: Group Gratuity Scheme Background ⮚ Gratuity Act, 1972 mandates that employers with at least 10 employees must pay gratuity to employees who have completed 5 years of employment. The amount is linked to last drawn salary x years of service, up to a maximum cap. ⮚ It is paid at time of employee exit, retirement, or due to death or total disability whilst employed ⮚ Organizations therefore need to create reserves to meet gratuity liability based on tenure of workforce Group Gratuity Schemes ⮚ Are offered by insurance companies to organizations, to meet this future liability ⮚ Premiums received from the employer are invested in different equity and debt funds to provide market linked returns over a long term. ⮚ The corpus, thus created, is used by the employer to make claim payments for gratuity when employees retire or leave.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: market linked returns over a long term. ⮚ The corpus, thus created, is used by the employer to make claim payments for gratuity when employees retire or leave. ⮚ The premiums paid by the employer are considered as a business expense, allowing it to reduce taxable business income. ⮚ At the end of each financial year most insurers review the employee data for changes in salaries, exits and new appointments. By this, they revalue the liability based on such changes Role of Insurance ⮚ Since likelihood of employees leaving or facing life-threatening situations deals with uncertainty, insurance can underwrite this like any other risk. Page 32: Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP) Background ⮚ Provide protection, investment and income-tax benefits (subject to lock-in period) ⮚ A portion of the premium is invested in market-linked funds, while the rest is used to provide life insurance coverage. Features ⮚ do not guarantee cash values. Instead, the value depends on the market price of the units held under the policy at the time of redemption/maturity.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: . Features ⮚ do not guarantee cash values. Instead, the value depends on the market price of the units held under the policy at the time of redemption/maturity. ⮚ As financial needs of the investor change over time he can vary his insurance coverage and the investment mix. He may also top-up his investments, make withdrawals and switch funds ⮚ Premiums paid for ULIPs are eligible for tax deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961 upto a maximum of Rs. 1,50,000. However, such plans must be kept in force for a period of 5 years to claim deduction ⮚ under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, if the premium paid on the policy is up to 10% of the sum assured during the entire term of the policy, the amount received on maturity is exempt from tax. Critique ⮚ the premium of ULIPs remains constant throughout the life of the policy, but the cost of insurance cover increases year by year as one gets older.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: x. Critique ⮚ the premium of ULIPs remains constant throughout the life of the policy, but the cost of insurance cover increases year by year as one gets older. ⮚ Hence a larger share of the premium gets assigned to life cover, and residual amount for investment reduces over time Page 33: ULIP – Investment options & payouts ⮚ ULIP investments are subject to the capital markets risk and any such risks are borne by the policyholder. Assets include Equity Debt Hybrid Money Market Switch Option Upon Policy Surrender: If surrendered during lock-in period of 5 years, the insurer pays the surrender value after deducting all the applicable charges from the fund value. If surrendered after lock-in period, but before the maturity date, the insurer pays the fund value (i.e., NAV (on the surrender date) * number of units held. Upon Maturity: The fund value as derived by multiplying the NAV (on the maturity date) by the number of units held is paid to the policyholder.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: r of units held. Upon Maturity: The fund value as derived by multiplying the NAV (on the maturity date) by the number of units held is paid to the policyholder. Upon death of Policyholder: If during the policy term, the nominee gets the higher of the Sum Assured or the Fund Value as a death benefit. E.g., if fund value is lower than the sum-assured, then the sum assured will be paid. Page 34: Contingency Planning ⮚ Key features: ⮚ Waiver of premium: The premiums payable can be a lump sum (i.e. single premium) or paid frequently (i.e. recurring). The amount payable will depend on the chosen sum assured and the insurer. The policy tenure can be for 10 years and above whereby dependent children from just born to a maximum of age of 25, with certain exceptions, are covered. The maturity amount of a policy can be chosen based on personal requirements. Money-back child life insurance plans offer a segmented payout method, which can both be in a lump sum or in yearly instalments. In ULIPs, the policyholder can withdraw partial amounts to cover financial difficulties.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: ayout method, which can both be in a lump sum or in yearly instalments. In ULIPs, the policyholder can withdraw partial amounts to cover financial difficulties. Applies to a child education plan. If the policyholder (the parent) dies within a stipulated period, the insurance company waives off all future premiums until the maturity of the policy. This rider enables the policy to continue without any breaks and passes the financial burden of the premium to the insurer. This ensures the maturity benefit set for a certain age remains intact as planned, in addition to the death benefit paid. Page 35: Policy Revival Schemes Revival Ordinary: Pay the arrears in one go Special: Re-initiate the policy by calculating a new premium (as per policyholder age). Ignores arrears. Revival of non-linked policies within 5 years and unit linked policies within 3 years of first unpaid Instalment revival Arrears under ordinary revival are converted to instalments. These are paif alongside the regular (old) premiums, e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: years of first unpaid Instalment revival Arrears under ordinary revival are converted to instalments. These are paif alongside the regular (old) premiums, e.g. quarterly, annuals Loan revival The insured person can take a policy loan to pay for arrears. The loan amount is adjusted in premiums. Until the revival date, the amount of loan is calculated as if the policy is within a forced condition. Reinstatement Reinstatement is the process by which the insurer puts back into force a policy that has either been terminated because of non-payment of premiums or falls under one of the non-forfeiture provisions.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: he insurer puts back into force a policy that has either been terminated because of non-payment of premiums or falls under one of the non-forfeiture provisions. The insured will have to submit proof of continued insurability, and pay all arrears and revival charges Page 36: Policy Surrender & Policy Assignment ⮚ Surrender = exit from policy before its maturity, usually after premiums have been paid for 3 continuous years ⮚ Surrender Value= Policyholder received some part of the total accumulated bonus and premiums paid ⮚ Guaranteed surrender value: ▪ guarantees a fixed percentage of paid premiums depending on the number of years the policy was continued; ▪ Value is the total premiums paid less any Extra Premium /Rider Premium * applicable Guarantee % less any survival benefits already paid. ⮚ Special surrender value: ▪ In this case, the surrender value includes sum assured, bonuses and policy term. No surrender value is available on Rider premiums, if any. ⮚ Assignment - transfer of the right, title and interest of the policy from one person (assignor) to another (assignee).

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: is available on Rider premiums, if any. ⮚ Assignment - transfer of the right, title and interest of the policy from one person (assignor) to another (assignee). ⮚ Governed by Section 38 of the Insurance Laws (Amendment) Act, 2015 ⮚ Executed by endorsement of original policy, or through a separate deed of assignment. The first assignment can be made only by the policyholder. ⮚ An assignment can neither be cancelled nor can the policy be reverted to the assignor unless the assignee reassigns the policy. The assignee is not entitled to increase the death benefit. Page 37: Policy Claims At maturity Is paid in full (e.g., Endowment, Whole Life) if the policy has been continued properly. Payout is tax-free In case of money-back plans that have periodic payouts, maturity payout is sum assured less periodic payment made. On death is payable if policy premiums are paid up-to-date or the death (or terminal/critical illness in some cases) occurs within the grace period.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 2
chunks.text: yment made. On death is payable if policy premiums are paid up-to-date or the death (or terminal/critical illness in some cases) occurs within the grace period. Payment can be lump sum or converted to an annuity, favouring the nominee or legal heir (in absence of nominee) Presumed dead Sections 107 and 108 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 state that individuals unheard of for seven years, are presumed to be dead. Insurers require a court decree to this effect (from the nominee) but can sometime approve claim based on strong circumstantial evidence. In such a cover, the insured is liable to pay for: ▪ Death or injury to any person, including the occupants of the motor vehicle ▪ Damage of property which does not belong to the insured ⮚ In case of a third party liability, the policyholder must immediately inform the insurance company of the incidence likely to give rise to liability claim. ⮚ A claim form, duly filled in, along with copies of Registration Certificate, Diving License, FIR are to be submitted to the insurance company at the earliest. ⮚ If any summons is received from Court, the same should also be sent to the insurance company immediately.

Personal Liability • Covers against legal liability • An additional cover beyond Vehicle or Homeowner policies • as these have low limits & exclusions 2.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 3

Professional Liability • Cover against professional liability • Malpractice (physical) • Errors and omissions (financial harm) • Premiums are high due to high risks • Legal liability arises from the laws of a land • Laws are typically based on one of three systems as defined by that jurisdiction • Civil / Common / Religious law • Legal liability typically refers to Civil law (from an insurance perspective) Page 25: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Key terms / basis for Liability insurance • Tort: The causing of harm (physical, emotional, financial) to another • Intentional: Deliberate harm (e.g. assault, battery, libel etc), not covered by insurance • Unintentional: Accidental financial harm usually via negligence, covered by insurance • Negligence: Not exercising a reasonable degree of care. • For this to apply as a Tort action, there must be financial loss or harm.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 3
chunks.text: ence, covered by insurance • Negligence: Not exercising a reasonable degree of care. • For this to apply as a Tort action, there must be financial loss or harm. • A person may have done no wrong, but can still be liable, e.g. a renter gets hurt on your property for no fault of yours. • Contributory negligence: When an individual contributes to his or her own loss. • Absolute Liability: When there is no way to show negligence. • E.g. dog owner has absolute liability if his or her pet dog bites someone randomly • Vicarious Liability: When one person is help liable for another person’s act. E.g. parent vicariously liable for their child causing financial harm to someone • Assumption of risk: Applies when individual accepts potential for risk but continues • e.g. extreme sports Page 26: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Civil and Criminal liability • Civil liability is the result of financial loss being caused by one individual to another • Criminal liability is the result of violation of a jurisdictions laws, and may result in prosecution and legal penalties (e.g.

It is applicable to every establishment as specified in Schedule I of the Act and employing 20 or more persons. ⮚ The Act is administered by the Government through the Employees' Provident Fund Organization (EPFO) which works under the Ministry of Labour and Employment. ⮚ EPFO provides for the provident fund, family pension and deposit-linked insurance for the employees in factories and other establishments.

Out of the 26 weeks, if desired, a woman can claim up to 8 weeks before the delivery. In case of a miscarriage, a woman can claim 6 weeks of paid leave with average pay from the date of miscarriage. Gratuity comes under the Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972. ⮚ The gratuity scheme is available to people working in factories, mines, oilfields, plantations, ports, railway companies and shops or establishments in which 10 or more persons are employed or have been employed on any day of the last 12 months. ⮚ Gratuity is payable to an employee (or to a nominee in case of his death) who has rendered continuous service of 5 years or more at the time of his termination of employment, superannuation, retirement or resignation. ⮚ However, completion of continuous service of 5 years is not necessary where the termination of employment is due to death or disablement due to accident or disease.

They are only liable for timely and precise processing of forms, paperwork and premiums, say for example in motor insurance. They have no duty to conduct a thorough examination of an individual’s business or to ensure that appropriate health insurance cover has been provided. Rather, it is the policyholder’s duty to ensure that he has purchased the required insurance cover. ⮚ On the other hand, brokers function as a super-independent agent. They have a higher duty towards the policyholders as they offer a host of insurance products and services to them. They analyze a business, interpret the data from various databases and secure correct and adequate cover for the insurance business. S. No. Agent Broker 1. Most localities have one or more GP’s • Diagnostics: Tests and other checks performed to diagnose the illness, usually prescribed by Physicians to start with • Specialists: recommended by Physicians, e.g. Heart specialist, ENT specialist etc • Medicines: Prescribed by any medical practitioner • Hospitalization: Arises when illness requires services that are provided in a hospital environment, e.g. surgery • Surgery: Act of being operated on by a surgeon.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 4
chunks.text: spitalization: Arises when illness requires services that are provided in a hospital environment, e.g. surgery • Surgery: Act of being operated on by a surgeon. Typically requires post-care during the recovery phase Page 3: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Types of Health Insurance • Two broad types of Policies are available • They both work on a similar approach of buying an insurance policy Basic Medical Expense Policies • Cover doctor visits at office or the hospital, laboratory services and other care outside the hospital • Do not generally cover additional or major expenses Comprehensive Medical Coverage • Cover large medical expenses associated with major illness or injury • Cover almost everything, up to certain scheduled benefit limits • Costs are contained via Deductibles, Coinsurance and Co-payments Page 4: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Cost aspect of Health policies Deductible • The amount the policy holder must pay before the plan pays anything • Once paid, it is done. • Certain plans include services that are always available (e.g.

chunks.title: Cfp Risk Lecture 4
chunks.text: • The amount the policy holder must pay before the plan pays anything • Once paid, it is done. • Certain plans include services that are always available (e.g. baby care) Coinsurance • Is a % of the expenses paid by the Insurer after deductible has been met. • E.g. if expense is 50K and deductible was 10K, then coinsurance % is applied on 50-10 = 40K Co-payments • A set amount that Insurer will pay for a service, e.g. a doctor visit of Rs 1000 • Does not depend on Deductible or Coinsurance Maximum out-of-pocket (MOOP) is a cap on the total expense payable by the Insured. E.g. Beyond 30000, insurer will pay all costs Page 5: www.proschoolonline.com www.proschoolonline.co m Managed Healthcare Plans • Fixed fee paid to physicians per plan subscriber that names that physician as primary care giver • e.g. locality physician has 10 such patients, so gets 10 x Fixed fee.

Care is provided case to case • Negotiated fee reductions are used for specialists Capitation • Fixed premium provides access to a healthcare network provided by the insurer • Here, Insurer is both the financier and the provider of healthcare • Physicians are gatekeepers, and guide policyholders within this network for further healthcare Health Maintenance Organization HMO • Business entity formed by medical professionals and hospitals • They contract with an Insurer and negotiate increased business in return for lower fees • Policy holders can choose to use this network or go elsewhere (and pay the difference) Preferred Provider Organization PPO • Use gatekeeper approach (like HMO) to keep costs down • Like PPO, policyholder decides where to go for treatment, and benefits vary accordingly Point Of Service POS • These arose in the USA to address rising cost of healthcare and are now widespread Page 6: www.proschoolonline.com

If policyholder is unable to perform any of these, they are eligible for benefits. E.g., mental ailments like Alzheimer's triggers this benefit • Coverage amount typically is a per day reimbursement based on the premium value. This amount can be set by the advisor based on nursing cost in each location • Elimination period usually the first 90 days from when an ailment is diagnosed. During this period, the individual has to pay all expenses for care • Benefit period the policy period following the elimination period when the insurer pays. Typically range from 2 to 10 years and benefit period is adjusted as follows. • Policy is for $100 per day, i.e. $3000 per month and has benefit period of 5 years. Hence total benefit pool is 3000 * 12 * 5 = $180,000. If the benefit is not used, the benefit period is extended. Similarly, if a smaller amount is used (e.g.
