chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: Page 1: CFA www.proschoolonline.com TAX PLANNING Page 2: Types of Taxes to Which Individuals Are Subject 1. Employment-related Taxes: • Taxes on earned income by national, regional, and local governments. 2. Value-added Taxes (VAT): • Consumption tax on each production step and at the sale. Rates vary from 10% to 20%. 3. Sales Taxes: • Imposed at the point of sale on retail goods and services by various governments. Different from VAT as it’s collected from consumers at sale. 4. Property Taxes: • Based on the assessed value of real property. 5. Excise Taxes: • Assessed on specific products. Can be a fixed percentage (ad valorem) or a fixed amount (specific). 6. Estate Transfer/Wealth Distribution Taxes: • Imposed on the distribution of estate assets www.proschoolonline.com Page 3: Transfer Pricing • Transfer pricing involves transactions of goods or services between divisions or departments within the same company.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: choolonline.com Page 3: Transfer Pricing • Transfer pricing involves transactions of goods or services between divisions or departments within the same company. www.proschoolonline.com ENTITY ENTITY LOCATED IN HIGH TAX COUNTRY LOCATED IN LOW TAX COUNTRY To Avoid Taxation With The Use Of Transfer Pricing Tax Implications: • Significant impact on the profit and tax liabilities of the involved organizations. • Territories are establishing strict guidelines around transfer pricing to address these issues. NOTE: Arriving at appropriate intra company transfer pricing can be difficult, and is beyond the scope of this course. Page 4: Tax Terms 1. Total Income : All income before exclusions. 2. Adjustments to Income : Items that increase or decrease income. 3. Exemption : Excludes income from tax calculation. 4. Deduction : Reduces taxable income for expenses. 5. Adjusted Gross Income : Income after adjustments. 6. Filing Status : Status affecting tax rate. 7. Marginal Tax Bracket : Rate on highest taxable income. 8. Phase-out : Reduction of deductions based on income. 9.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: Filing Status : Status affecting tax rate. 7. Marginal Tax Bracket : Rate on highest taxable income. 8. Phase-out : Reduction of deductions based on income. 9. Source : Where income is earned, impacting tax. 10. Tax Credit : Direct reduction of tax owed. 11. Tax Avoidance : Legal tax reduction strategies. www.proschoolonline.com Page 5: Tax Terms 12. Tax Deferral : Postponing tax payment. 13. Tax Evasion : Illegal avoidance of tax payments. 14. Tax Return : Document reporting income and tax. 15. Tax Treaty : Agreement on cross-border taxation. www.proschoolonline.com A financial planner who is helping a client with his or her tax liability will be well-served to have a more complete knowledge of relevant tax terminology and tax experts they can collaborate with. Page 6: Property-Related Taxes • Includes all owned items, tangible (e.g., jewelry, real estate) or intangible (e.g., stocks). Types of Property: Personal (e.g., jewelry, furniture), Real (e.g., land, houses), Investments or Securities (e.g., stocks, bonds).

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: r intangible (e.g., stocks). Types of Property: Personal (e.g., jewelry, furniture), Real (e.g., land, houses), Investments or Securities (e.g., stocks, bonds). • Tax Implications: Some regions with low income tax may compensate with higher property or use taxes. • Strategic Planning: Higher-income individuals often have more options to manage tax liabilities through income shifting or deferral. • Types of Property: We can highlight three distinct categories of property: 1. Personal 2. Real 3. Investments or securities www.proschoolonline.com NOTE: Stock certificate, don’t be fooled into thinking it’s a tangible item. It still fits into the intangible category, because the piece of paper merely represents the intangible item. The paper has no tangible value by itself Page 7: Capital Assets: Gains and Losses Capital Assets: Used to generate financial returns, distinct from business-use assets like inventory or receivables. Capital Gains and Losses: Realized upon sale, categorized separately for tax treatment.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: eturns, distinct from business-use assets like inventory or receivables. Capital Gains and Losses: Realized upon sale, categorized separately for tax treatment. Tax Treatment: Often favorable compared to ordinary income tax rates in many territories. Types of Capital Gains/Losses: 1. Realized : Gains or losses realized upon sale. 2. Unrealized : Gains or losses based on asset value changes without sale. 3. Short-term : Held for less than one year, typically taxed at higher rates. 4. Long-term : Held for one year or more, usually taxed at lower rates. www.proschoolonline.com Capital Gains/ Losses Realized Long - Term Unrealized Short- Term Capital Gains Or Losses May Be Categorized In Four Ways: Page 8: How Accounting Fits Into the Tax World Accounting and Taxation Connection: • Accountants ensure compliance with tax laws and standards. • They follow accepted accounting principles, crucial for accurate financial reporting. Importance of Consistent Standards: • Consistency prevents economic chaos and ensures reliable financial data.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: ciples, crucial for accurate financial reporting. Importance of Consistent Standards: • Consistency prevents economic chaos and ensures reliable financial data. • Facilitates accurate analysis, decision-making, and investor confidence. Benefits of Consistent Standards: • Enhances transparency, comparability, and accountability across organizations. • Supports clients in understanding financial status and investors in evaluating opportunities. Role for Financial Planners: • Understanding basic accounting principles aids collaboration with accountants. • Enables comprehensive client advice on tax planning and compliance. www.proschoolonline.com Page 9: General Accounting Principles Around the Globe • Two main accounting standards guide just about all territories. 1. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) in the U.S. and similar standards elsewhere. 2. International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) used in most other countries for transparency and efficiency.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: in the U.S. and similar standards elsewhere. 2. International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) used in most other countries for transparency and efficiency. • IFRS standards are designed to promote transparency, accountability, and economic efficiency, and are permitted or required by at least 125 jurisdictions (IFRS, 2017). www.proschoolonline.com IFRS Standards Examples: IFRS STANDARDS Conceptual framework for financial reporting First-time adoption of IFRS Business combinations Insurance contracts Financial instruments: Disclosures Operating segments Consolidated financial statements Fair value measurement Leases Page 10: Organizational Ownership Structures 1. Public Sector Organizations: Operate under government regulation and taxation structures. 2. Private Sector Organizations: • Individual Ownership: Simplest form where profits and losses directly affect the owner's income. • Partnerships: Multiple individuals share profits and losses directly. • Joint Stock Companies: Raise capital through shares; can be private or public limited.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: • Partnerships: Multiple individuals share profits and losses directly. • Joint Stock Companies: Raise capital through shares; can be private or public limited.  Private limited: Shares held by members, not publicly traded.  Public limited: Shares offered to the public, with regulatory oversight. 3. Other Structures: Various forms exist to limit liability and manage ownership diversity. 4. Regular Corporations: Diverse in structure and ownership, ranging from closely held to publicly traded. www.proschoolonline.com We will not explore the specific tax ramifications of corporate organizational structures, because of their great diversity and variations within territories. Page 11: Cross-Border www.proschoolonline.com Types of Taxpayers: 1. Foreign Nationals/Expatriates : Individuals living and working outside their home territory, subject to tax laws of both their host and home countries. They may benefit from foreign tax credits or income exclusions. 2.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: king outside their home territory, subject to tax laws of both their host and home countries. They may benefit from foreign tax credits or income exclusions. 2. Residents Abroad : Former residents who maintain assets or income sources in their previous territory, subject to tax liabilities there. Tax Compliance: • Compliance includes reporting all income and assets to the relevant tax authorities, following local tax laws, and obtaining necessary certifications before leaving a territory. Taxation of Assets: • Real estate, retirement funds, and investment portfolios may be subject to different tax rules depending on their location and the taxpayer's residency status. Page 12: Cross-Border www.proschoolonline.com Complexities and Challenges: • Cross-border tax situations can be complex due to varying tax regimes, treaties, and compliance requirements. • Seeking professional advice is crucial to avoid mistakes and legal liabilities. Ethical Considerations: • Attempting to evade taxes is illegal and unethical.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: • Seeking professional advice is crucial to avoid mistakes and legal liabilities. Ethical Considerations: • Attempting to evade taxes is illegal and unethical. • Enhanced international cooperation and technology make tax evasion increasingly difficult and risky. Page 13: Source Rules: Interest and Dividend Income www.proschoolonline.com 1. Interest Income : Source Determination: Interest income is generally sourced at the payer's place of residence. 1. Individuals, Partnerships, Trusts : Sourced where the payer resides. 2. Corporations : Sourced at the place of incorporation. • Domestic Payers: Interest paid by resident individuals, partnerships, trusts, and corporations in your territory is considered domestic income. • Foreign Payers: Interest paid by foreign corporations is considered foreign income, typically sourced from the corporation's domicile. • Complex Cases: If a foreign corporation has a major office in your territory, interest income may be sourced from either territory based on specific rules. 2.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: • Complex Cases: If a foreign corporation has a major office in your territory, interest income may be sourced from either territory based on specific rules. 2. Dividend Income : • Source: Generally from the corporation's place of incorporation. May change if significant income is from another territory. Page 14: Source Rules: Credits www.proschoolonline.com Income from personal services is sourced where the services are performed. Complex Scenarios: • Periodic Services in Foreign Territories : Individuals performing services in multiple territories face source determination challenges. • Example: National Hockey League (NHL) teams and players earning income from games played in multiple territories. • Employer Income: Income of employers from employee activities in foreign territories can be complex to source. • Professional Advice: Due to complexities, financial planners should refer clients to experts for cross- border income issues. • Client Awareness: Planners should inform clients about potential difficulties with cross-border income to manage expectations and compliance.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: r income issues. • Client Awareness: Planners should inform clients about potential difficulties with cross-border income to manage expectations and compliance. Page 15: www.proschoolonline.com Retirement – Pension Plan Distributions Territories offer qualified retirement plans with tax benefits, either tax-free or tax-deferred. Single Territory Simplicity: • If all retirement benefits are within one territory, the tax rules are straightforward. Multiple Territories Complexity: • Having retirement benefits in multiple territories complicates the tax situation as different territories have different tax rules for retirement benefits. Tax Treaties: • Tax treaties may define how benefits are taxed but are not always comprehensive. • Benefits are typically paid in the current territory of residence. Distribution Taxation: • Plan distributions may be taxed by the current territory of residence or the territory where the plan originated. • Taxes may be withheld in both territories, requiring tax return filings to recover withheld taxes.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: ry of residence or the territory where the plan originated. • Taxes may be withheld in both territories, requiring tax return filings to recover withheld taxes. Page 16: www.proschoolonline.com Retirement – Pension Plan Distributions Lump Sum vs. Periodic Distributions : • Lump sum distributions are often taxable and may follow different rules than periodic payments. Plan Qualification: • A retirement plan qualified in one territory may not be considered qualified in another, affecting the tax status of earnings. Professional Advice: • Due to the complexity, consulting with a cross-border tax professional is highly recommended for accurate tax planning. Page 17: Planned Approaches www.proschoolonline.com Taxes can be legally assessed, and clients must usually pay some portion. Focus: Strategic planning to control the amount of tax levied and its payment. Simple Strategies: • Properly completing and submitting forms (e.g., VAT refunds). • Methods to reduce or eliminate double taxation through legal agreements between jurisdictions.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: • Properly completing and submitting forms (e.g., VAT refunds). • Methods to reduce or eliminate double taxation through legal agreements between jurisdictions. Double Taxation: • Occurs when two jurisdictions tax the same income. • Coordination required to prevent double taxation, especially in economic unions like the EU. Economic Cooperation: • Unified financial markets and coordinated tax strategies in economic unions. • Example: VAT replacing sales and turnover taxes in European territories. Page 18: Tax Systems Overview www.proschoolonline.com Jurisdiction Basis: • Nationality: Taxing all income of citizens or domestic companies regardless of where it's earned. • Territoriality: Taxing all entities earning money within the territory, focusing on the income source. • Source Jurisdiction: Imposing tax on income like royalties, investment income, rent earned by nonresidents. Primary and Secondary Taxing Authority : • Primary Authority: Place of territorial connection (where the income is earned). • Secondary Authority: Territory of nationality or residence.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: ing Authority : • Primary Authority: Place of territorial connection (where the income is earned). • Secondary Authority: Territory of nationality or residence. • Avoiding Double Taxation: Secondary authority usually credits taxes paid in the primary territory. Tax Collection Practice: • Territories typically collect taxes only on income with which they have a connection. Page 19: Offshore Investing Offshore investing involves placing money in jurisdictions outside the investor's country of residence. Considerations and Risks: • Legality: Differentiates between tax avoidance (legal) and tax evasion (illegal). • Legislative Changes: Laws may change, potentially making previously legal activities illegal. • Reallocation Concerns: Changing laws can tie up funds in litigation, causing access issues. • Coordination: Essential to coordinate strategies to mitigate risks such as increased taxation and lack of access to funds.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: litigation, causing access issues. • Coordination: Essential to coordinate strategies to mitigate risks such as increased taxation and lack of access to funds. www.proschoolonline.com BENEFITS Anonymity and/or privacy Asset protection and limited liability Tax savings Lower levels of regulations Page 20: Offshore Investing www.proschoolonline.com Tax-Free Zones: Certain territories offer zones with no excise tax, beneficial for small business owners. These zones are generally viewed as legal and legitimate. Strategic Planning: • Evaluate Legality: Ensure offshore strategies comply with current laws. • Prepare for Changes: Anticipate and plan for potential legislative shifts. • Access to Funds: Consider the risk of funds becoming inaccessible due to legal disputes or repatriation processes. Page 21: Income Shifting Income shifting involves transferring income from a person in a higher tax bracket to someone in a lower tax bracket. Common Implementation: • Often done by gifting money from a parent to a child.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: g income from a person in a higher tax bracket to someone in a lower tax bracket. Common Implementation: • Often done by gifting money from a parent to a child. Benefits: • Tax Reduction: Reduces taxable income for the person in a higher tax bracket, potentially lowering their overall tax liability. • Estate Planning: Allows for the transfer of substantive gifts to children or other family members, reducing the taxable estate of the donor. www.proschoolonline.com Page 22: Family Law  The way in which payments to support an ex-spouse and children are treated for tax purposes varies by territory, but there are some consistencies.  Normally, child support (i.e., ongoing payment for expenses related to rearing children) is not considered to be income to the custodial (receiving) parent.  Such payments almost never qualify as a tax-deductible expense to the payer either.  Often, the mother is awarded child custody, at least until the child reaches a certain age, and the father is responsible for making support payments.

chunks.title: Tax Planning

GST increases the tax base by minimizing the various exemptions provided by Central and State Government GST subsumes various indirect tax levies in India. Considering the federal structure of India, DUAL STRUCTURE OF GST was implemented in India. GST ensures free flow of goods across the country. Liquor for Human Consumption and petroleum products have been kept outside the ambit of GST. A director employee - who is a director in the employer company at any time during the previous year, is a specified employee of the company in which he is a director. 2. An employee who has substantial interest in the employer company - An employee who is a beneficial owner of equity shares carrying 20% or more voting power has substantial interest in the employer- company. 3. An employee drawing in excess of Rs. 50,000 - An employee (not covered by the above two categories), whose income chargeable under the head "Salaries" exceeds Rs. 50,000, is a specified employee. Valuation of Fair Market Value : Listed Company: • Fair market value is the average of the opening and closing prices on the date of exercising the option, on the recognized stock exchange where the share is most traded. • If no trading on the exercise date, use the closing price on the nearest preceding date. • If multiple exchanges trade the stock, use the exchange with the highest trading volume. Unlisted Company: • Fair market value determined by a merchant banker on the date of exercise of the option. Page 115: Interest on Securities (other than dividend from Indian companies) www.proschoolonline.com Taxation of Dividends: General Rule: • Dividend income from Indian companies is taxable under "Income from Other Sources" at slab rates (effective from 01-04-2020). Tax Deduction at Source (TDS): • Domestic companies must deduct 10% TDS on dividends exceeding ₹5,000 per financial year under Section 194. • The dividend up to ₹5,000 is not exempt from tax; all dividend income must be reported and taxed accordingly. Deemed Accrual: • Dividend paid by an Indian company is considered to accrue or arise in India and is limited to the amount of the company's accumulated profits. Page 116: Gifts www.proschoolonline.com • Gifts (money/property) received without or with inadequate consideration exceeding ₹50,000 are taxable as 'Income from Other Sources'.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: www.proschoolonline.com • Gifts (money/property) received without or with inadequate consideration exceeding ₹50,000 are taxable as 'Income from Other Sources'. • Applicable to individuals, Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) (after Oct 1, 2009), and all persons (after Apr 1, 2017). Exemptions: 1. Gifts from relatives. 2. Gifts received on the occasion of an individual's marriage. 3. Gifts received by will/inheritance. 4. Gifts received in contemplation of death. 5. Gifts from local authorities. 6. Gifts from certain educational, medical, and charitable institutions (Section 10(23C), Section 12A/12AA). 7. Shares received due to demerger/amalgamation (from AY 2017-18). Page 117: Gifts www.proschoolonline.com 1. Gifts of Cash and Kind: • Aggregate value of gifts up to ₹50,000 in a year is exempt. • All transactions within the year are summed to determine the total amount. 2. Gifts of Movable Assets: • If the total fair market value of movable assets received without consideration exceeds ₹50,000, the entire amount is taxable.

Gifts of Movable Assets: • If the total fair market value of movable assets received without consideration exceeds ₹50,000, the entire amount is taxable. • If received for consideration less than fair market value by more than ₹50,000, the difference is taxable. Valuation: • Purchased from Registered Dealer: Invoice value is the fair market value. • Received by Other Modes: Price it would fetch in the open market on the valuation date. ₹50,000 limit considers the total of all transactions in the year, not individual transactions. He is also a member of an HUF, which has an income of Rs 4,00,000 for the year 2023-24. Out of this, Atul gets Rs 2,00,000 as his share of income. This Rs 2,00,000 will be exempt in the hands of Atul by virtue of Section 10(2) and he will pay tax only on his income of Rs 7,00,000. Exemption: Any sum received by an individual as a member of a Hindu Undivided Family (HUF) from the HUF's income or estate is exempt from tax. Page 131: Sukanya Samridhhi Scheme (Sec. 10(11A)) : Salient features of the scheme are as below: • Eligibility: For a girl child up to 10 years old; one account per child, maximum two accounts. • Contribution Limit: Minimum Rs 250; maximum Rs 1,50,000 per year. • Interest: Compounded yearly, government-notified rate. • Withdrawals: Allowed for education expenses upon attaining 18 years, up to 50% of the preceding year's balance. • Maturity: At 21 years from account opening or on marriage, whichever is earlier. • Tax Benefits: Contributions, interest, and withdrawals are tax-exempt.

Key Notes: • Consistent Clubbing: If a child’s income is clubbed with a parent, it continues to be clubbed with that parent in subsequent years regardless of changes in the parents' incomes. • Separated Parents: If parents are separated, the child's income is clubbed with the parent maintaining the child. • Orphaned Child: If both parents are deceased, the minor’s income is not clubbed with any other relative’s income. • Exemption: Parents can claim an exemption of Rs 1,500 per minor child’s income. If the income is less than Rs 1,500, the exemption is limited to the income amount.

Page 156: Clubbing of Income What is Clubbing of Income Provision? • Clubbing of income refers to including income from specified persons into the income of another taxpayer for tax computation purposes. • Inclusion of Loss: Even if the income is negative (loss), clubbing provisions apply, and the loss is included in the taxpayer's income. www.proschoolonline.com Clubbing provisions are contained in section 60 to 64 of the Income Tax Act. Page 157: Clubbing of Income Transfer Of Income Without Transfer Of Asset (Sec 60) www.proschoolonline.com  In cases when a taxpayer retains the ownership of an asset, but decides to transfer its income by doing an agreement or any other way, the Act will still consider that income as his income and it will be added to his total income for taxation purposes.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: me by doing an agreement or any other way, the Act will still consider that income as his income and it will be added to his total income for taxation purposes. CONDITIONS 1 The taxpayer owns an asset 2 The ownership of the asset is not transferred by him (he has retained the ownership of the asset). 3 Income from the asset is transferred to any person under a settlement, trust, covenant, agreement arrangement. 4 The above transfer may or may not be revocable. 5 The above transfer may have been effected at any time. If the above conditions are satisfied, the income from the asset would be taxable in the hands of the transferor. Inter-source adjustment under the same head of income Page 160: Business Loss And Depreciation www.proschoolonline.com Carry forward and set-off of business loss other than speculation loss (Sec. 72) • Loss from a business (excluding speculative business) can be carried forward if not fully adjusted in the year incurred. • It can only be set off against income under "Profits and gains of business or profession" in subsequent years. • Must file the return on time (Section 139(1)) to carry forward the loss. • Carry forward period: 8 assessment years; no limit for specified businesses. Unabsorbed Depreciation: • Depreciation deducted from "Profits and gains of business or profession". • Unabsorbed depreciation carried forward to subsequent years. • Added to depreciation for the following year if not fully absorbed. Page 161: Speculation Loss (Sec. 73) www.proschoolonline.com • Set-Off in Same Year: Loss from speculation business can only be set off against profits from another speculation business in the same assessment year. • Carry Forward: If not fully set off, can be carried forward to subsequent years for set-off against income from any speculation business. • Carry Forward Period: Up to 4 assessment years immediately following the year in which the loss was first computed. • Return Filing: Must file the return before the due date to carry forward such loss. NOTE: 1. The same speculation business doesn't need to continue in the assessment year in which the loss is set off. 2. Provisions of Section 72(2) regarding depreciation or capital expenditure on scientific research apply to speculation business as they do to other businesses.

Page 167: Tax Collected at Source (TCS) www.proschoolonline.com Under Section 206 C tax is collected at source in the following cases: Category A: Sale of Specified Goods (Liquor, Tendu Leaves, Timber, Scrap, Minerals): • Applicability: Sellers including Government, local authorities, corporations, firms, individuals, and HUFs are required to collect TCS from buyers for the sale of specified goods. • Tax Rate: The tax rates vary depending on the goods, ranging from 1% to 5%. • Collection Time: Tax is collected at the time of debiting the amount payable by the buyer to the seller's account or at the time of receipt of payment, whichever is earlier. Page 174: Admissible deductions – Net Income or Taxable Income • Special Nature Deductions: Allowed for specified categories of taxpayers. • Sections 80C to 80GGC: Deductions related to various investments and payments. • Sections 80-IA to 80U: Deductions for certain types of income. • Limitation: Total deductions under sections 80C to 80U cannot exceed the Gross Total Income. (Refer previous chps for a detailed study of provisions under sections 80C to 80U). www.proschoolonline.com Gross Total Income Deduction Under Sections 80C To 80U. STEPS TO BE FOLLOWED 1 Tax On Net Income 2 (Less): Rebate Under 87A 3 (Less): Income Tax After Rebate Under Section 87A [(1) - (2)] 4 Add: Surcharge 5 Find The Total [(3)+(4)] 6 Add: Health & Education Cess [4% Of (5)) 7 Find The Total Tax [(5)+(6)] 8 (Less): Rebate Under Section 86, 89, 90, 90A Or 91 9 Tax Liability [(7)-(8)] 10 Add: Interest/ Penalty, Etc. 11 (Less): Pre-paid 12 Tax Payable [(9) + (10) - (11)] Page 176: www.proschoolonline.com Tax As Per Slabs And Applicable Rates, Surcharge And Cess Note 2: Marginal relief is available from surcharge in following manner- Net Income Range Marginal Relief Exceeds Rs 50 Lakh but does not exceed Rs 1 Crore Tax and surcharge payable shall not exceed the tax on Rs 50 Lakh by more than the income exceeding Rs 50 Lakh. Exceeds Rs 1 Crore but does not exceed Rs 2 Crore Tax and surcharge payable shall not exceed the tax on Rs 1 Crore by more than the income exceeding Rs 1 Crore.

Page 177: Advance Tax Advance tax is a system where the assesse deposits tax in installments throughout the financial year instead of a lump sum at the end. www.proschoolonline.com Due Date Advance Tax Payable On or before June 15th 15% of advance tax On or before Sep 15th 45% of advance tax On or before Dec 15th 75% of advance tax On or before March 15th 100% of advance tax NOTES: 1. Advance tax is not required if the tax liability does not exceed Rs 10,000. 2. Senior citizens without business/professional income are exempt from paying advance tax. 3.

Payments made on or before March 31 are considered as advance tax for the financial year. Page 178: Advance Tax www.proschoolonline.com If the last day for an installment falls on a bank holiday, payment can be made on the next working day without incurring mandatory interest under sections 234B and 234C. Interest for Deferment (Section 234C) : Interest at 1% per month for 3 months is charged for deferment, except for the last installment on March 15, where it is 1% for one month. Page 179: Who should file Returns? www.proschoolonline.com Compulsory Filing Requirements: 1. Companies/Firms : All companies and firms must file a return, regardless of income or loss. 2. Individuals and HUFs : Must file if income exceeds exemption limits: • Rs 2,50,000 for individuals below 60 years. • Rs 3,00,000 for senior citizens (60-79 years). • Rs 5,00,000 for super senior citizens (80 years and above). 3.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: • Rs 2,50,000 for individuals below 60 years. • Rs 3,00,000 for senior citizens (60-79 years). • Rs 5,00,000 for super senior citizens (80 years and above). 3. Trusts and Charitable Organizations : Must file if income exceeds Rs 2,50,000 without claiming exemptions. 4. Political Parties : The Chief Executive Officer must file if the income exceeds Rs 2,50,000 without claiming exemptions. 5. Educational Institutions and Investment Funds : Universities, colleges, business trusts, and investment funds must file regardless of income or loss. Page 180: Appropriate Income Tax Return (ITR) Form www.proschoolonline.com Form No. Description ITR 1 (Sahaj) For individuals being a resident (other than not ordinarily resident) having total income up to Rs 50 lakh, having Income from Salaries, one house property, other sources (Interest etc), and agricultural income up to Rs 5,000. ITR 2 For individual and HUF not having income from profit and gains of Business or Profession. ITR 3 For individual and HUF having income from profit and gains of Business or Profession.

Some of the important documents required by an individual/HUF while filing his/her income tax return are: Page 182: Interest Payable On Default In Furnishing Return And Default In Payment Of Advance Tax www.proschoolonline.com Interest For Default In Filing The Income Tax Return (Section 234a): • If the income tax return is filed after the due date or not filed at all, simple interest at 1% per month or part thereof is applicable. • Interest is calculated from the day following the due date until the date of filing the return (if filed late) or until the end of the Assessment Year (if no return is filed). Interest For Default In Payment Of Advance Tax (Section 234B): • If the taxpayer fails to pay advance tax by the due dates or if the advance tax paid is less than 90% of the assessed tax, simple interest at 1% per month or part thereof applies.

chunks.title: Tax Planning
chunks.text: ls to pay advance tax by the due dates or if the advance tax paid is less than 90% of the assessed tax, simple interest at 1% per month or part thereof applies. • Interest is calculated from the due date of advance tax payment until the actual payment is made, based on the shortfall in the advance tax paid. Page 183: Revised Return and Tax Refunds Apart from penalty on late filing of income tax return, if the assessee makes a mistake while filing his tax returns, he has time till March 31 of the AY or before the completion of the assessment , whichever is earlier, to file his revised return. www.proschoolonline.com Tax Refunds: • A tax refund occurs when the taxes paid exceed the actual tax liability, including interest. • Refunds can arise from advance tax, self-assessment tax, tax deducted at source (TDS), foreign tax credits, etc.
